## Breaking Changes in Beta 7

Beta 7 comes with a lot of changes in the Customization API.

I think this post is only relevant if you use the Customization API.

### Render-functions moved

The default render functions moved from `sty.renderer` to `sty.renderfunc`.

### Changes within the customization API

Before `Beta 7` new rules were assigned like this:

```python
from sty import fg

# Direct attribute assignment
fg.orange = ('rgb', (255, 150, 50))

# Attribute assignment via method
fg.set('my_color', 'eightbit', 51) 
```
Since `Beta 7` you do it like this:

```python
from sty import fg, Rule, Render

# Direct attribute assignment
fg.orange = Rule(Render.rgb_fg, 255, 150, 50)

# Attribute assignment via method
fg.set_rule('my_color', Rule(Render.eightbit_fg, 51))
```

As you see there are a couple of changes.

#### `set` method renamed to `set_rule`
`fg.set(..` is now `fg.set_rule(...`

#### The `Rule` type
As you see in the example above, we now use the `Rule` type to set new styling/formatting rules for attributes. This makes for a cleaner `Base` class and a more structured API.

**The `Render` enum**
As you see in the example above, there is a new `Render` enum, which provides the renderer names. That means you can select a renderer via the `Render` enum:

 ```python
Rule(Render.rgb_fg, 10, 20, 30)
```

The old style of selecting renderers by string still works as well:

```python
Rule('rgb_fg', 10, 20, 30)
```

#### New register-class definition

Before `Beta 7` you could extend the default register classes like this:

```python
from sty.register import FgRegister

# Extend default Fg register.
class MyFgRegister(FgRegister):

    # Set a custom render-function
    def custom_rgb_fg(self, *args):
        return my_custom_rgb_fg_func(*args)

    # Set render-function for rgb call. 
    def _rgb_call(self, *args):
        return my_custom_rgb_fg_func(*args)

    black = ('sgr', 31)
    red = ('sgr', 34)
    orange = ('custom_rgb_fg', (255, 128, 0))
    # ...
```

In `Beta 7` you do it like this:

```python
import sty
from sty import FgRegister, Rule, Render

# Extend the enum with the name of your new render-funtion.
class Render(sty.Render):
    custom_rgb_fg = 'custom_rgb_fg'


# Extend default Fg register.
class MyFgRegister(FgRegister):

    # Set a custom render-function
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__() # Call super to apply render-functions from parent FgRegister.
        self.set_renderer(Render.custom_rgb_fg, my_custom_rgb_fg)

    # Set render-function for rgb call. 
    rgb_call = Rule(Render.rgb_fg)

    black = Rule(Render.sgr, 31)
    red = Rule(Render.sgr, 34)
    orange = Rule(Render.custom_rgb_bg, 255, 128, 0)
    # ...
```
As you see in the example above, they way we assign render-functions and the way we set render-functions for the special call methods has changed.

I suggest adding the name of the new renderer to the `Render` enum as shown above.
Done!

Of course the change has been shipped with proper tests. You will find them
in `tests35/`.

This is my solution:
```python
import pook
from pook.engine import MockEngine
from pook.interceptors.base import BaseInterceptor
import requests

from mocket.mocket import Mocket
from mocket.mockhttp import Entry, Response


class MocketPookEntry(Entry):
    pook_request = None
    pook_engine = None

    def can_handle(self, data):
        can_handle = super(MocketPookEntry, self).can_handle(data)

        if can_handle:
            self.pook_engine.match(self.pook_request)
        return can_handle

    @classmethod
    def single_register(cls, method, uri, body='', status=200, headers=None):
        entry = cls(uri, method, Response(body=body, status=status, headers=headers))
        Mocket.register(entry)
        return entry


class MocketInterceptor(BaseInterceptor):
    def activate(self):
        Mocket.disable()
        Mocket.enable()

    def disable(self):
        Mocket.disable()


class MocketEngine(MockEngine):

    def __init__(self, engine):
        # Store pook engine
        self.engine = engine
        # Store HTTP client interceptors
        self.interceptors = []
        # Self-register MocketInterceptor
        self.add_interceptor(MocketInterceptor)

    def activate(self):
        for mock in self.engine.mocks:

            request = mock._request
            method = request.method
            url = request.rawurl

            response = mock._response
            body = response._body
            status = response._status
            headers = response._headers

            entry = MocketPookEntry.single_register(method, url, body, status, headers)
            entry.pook_engine = self.engine
            entry.pook_request = request

        super(MocketEngine, self).activate()


@pook.activate
def test_my_api():
    pook.set_mock_engine(MocketEngine)
    mock = pook.get('http://twitter.com/api/1/foobar', body='{"a": 1}', headers={'content-type': 'application/json'}, reply=404, response_json={'error': 'foo'})

    resp = requests.get('http://twitter.com/api/1/foobar')
    assert resp.status_code == 404
    assert resp.json() == {'error': 'foo'}
    assert mock.calls == 1
```
The only thing still missing (something I've already experienced in the past) is `mock.calls` is not an integer, but your doc always compares it with an `int`. What's wrong with that?

Digging just a bit further, it seems that the declared dependency is in jaraco.functools, but more importantly, the issue lies with pip, which can't even be detected for wheel-based installs, so I think the best that could be done is to advertise the issue (which is done by this ticket and the sister ticket), to recommend that everyone upgrade their packaging tools.

I'll also mention that this issue only affects Python 2, so switching to Python 3 also corrects the issue.

Currently URH is in the repositories of Arch / Suse / Fedora and Gentoo. For other distributions the installation is as simple as ``` pip3 install urh ```. If you want to have all dependencies preinstalled you can use the URH [docker image](https://hub.docker.com/r/jopohl/urh/).

Of course, you need to have docker installed for this, which is not the case for an AppImage. However, I see different problems that would arise here: For example, if I install hackrf via ubuntu package there are automatically udev rules installed so I do not have to be root to access the USB device. On the other hand, even if we bundle hackrf driver in the AppImage we could not install udev rules via AppImage, so we would have to be root to run it when we want to access USB devices or install udev rules afterwards.

So if anyone wants to put work on this, I will accept a PR for it, but we have currently no plans to dive into this.

Hi,

Our team is working on the fork version of lupa named lutorpy, for interfacing between numpy and torch.Thank lupa for enabling us to have such a nice way to use lua in python.

For using numpy with torch, you have two function `tensor.asNumpyArray()` and `torch.fromNumpyArray()` to convert from/to numpy array. With `tensor.asNumpyArray()`, the memory is shared between numpy array and torch tensor if you have a contiguous tensor.

And there are other stuff I am working on to make it more easier to use torch in python, for example, we introduced "._" syntax for replacing ":" operator in lua, so you can use `tensor._size()` for accesing size(), just like `tensor:size()` in lua. With that you can easily convert lua code into python.

Check https://github.com/imodpasteur/lutorpy for more details.

Any kind of contribution are highly appreciated.

Ok, the Nix failure is one of those:

    why does this not work?
    ...1 hour later...
    how could have this ever worked?

When venv creates a virtualenv, all of the activate scripts have permission `444` (that is, read only for everyone). clonevirtualenv subsequently does a `shutil.copytree(`, which preserves the permission, and thus it fails when touching the scripts.

The old virtualenv tool instead creates the permissions as `644`, allowing the user to modify it. (I think the `444` is cleaner, since those scripts are never supposed to be modified)

I also reproduced this manually on Macos, by creating a venv in `/tmp`, and then invoking clonevirtualenv.

This was a bit frustrating to debug, since running instead the test inside Nix locally didn't trigger this (somehow, the permissions for the activate scripts are `644` just like for plain virtualenv). Also, since High Sierra the test collection of py.test seem to have slow down incredibly :/

Anyhow, this isn't a spurious error due to environment/configuration, so it should be fixed... either upstream in virtualenv-clone, or by ensuring that such activate scripts have write permission before attempting a copy of the virtualenv.

Oh, that's kind of evil.    I'm not exactly sure what a programmer writing that would expect the behavior to be since there's no way to actually know when control will return from a yield (if at all).    I'm not even sure how I'd detect something like that.

A few weeks or so back, I was considering making a PR that enabled async_threads to use proper async/await, but there was a reason I didn't, and I've just remembered: allowing `await` in an async_thread breaks code that depends on being in the same thread as the event loop.

Some of my code that I've written uses the assumption that the threading.local they use is for the same thread as the loop thread; doing an `await` from a worker thread to something that uses thread-local state will look up the wrong data (this issue also applies to PEP 567 ContextVars, to some degree). The old method of the AWAIT() function, however, does not have this issue because it submits all coroutines to the curio thread to be ran.

Not sure if this grounds to revert or if it's a design antipattern that you want people to avoid with curio libraries instead, but I thought I'd put that out there.

One option would be to use a [TaskGroup](http://curio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference.html#task-groups). you are getting the warning from curio because you aren't joining on the return of your spawn task. `TaskGroup` used as a context manager automatically does the joining for you.

There seems to be a kind of mythology that Curio is some sort of crazy "dabeaz" experimental project whereas Trio is a serious "production" version of it.   Aside from having "io" in the name and using async/await, there are few similarities between these two projects when it comes to internal design and implementation sensibilities.  Call Curio crazy if you want, but I've always considered it to be a serious project that people could actually use to do interesting things (although they probably shouldn't because they should be using threads instead).  Moreover, even if I had infinite time to work on it, Curio would still not evolve into Trio.     That said, I mostly wish people would just acknowledge that they are separate projects with different priorities and that one is not the "more serious" version of the other.

Thus
`psig = pound_force_per_square_inch; offset: 14.7 = pound_force_per_square_inch_gauge`
gives incorrect
```python
print (Q_(14.7,ureg('psig')).to(ureg.psi))
>>>4.264109489268151e-06 pound_force_per_square_inch
```

```python
print (Q_(0,ureg('psig')).to(ureg.psi))
>>>2.1320547446340754e-06 pound_force_per_square_inch
```

And
`psig = 6.895*10**6 * g/(m*s**2); offset: 14.7*6.895*10**6 = pound_force_per_square_inch_gauge`
gives correct
```python
print (Q_(14.7,ureg('psig')).to(ureg.psi))
>>>29.4010349285039 pound_force_per_square_inch
```
```python

>>> 14.700517464251948 pound_force_per_square_inch

Isn't the purpose of `six` backwards compatibility?  😃 

The context of this is that I'm developing a comprehensive list of moves and removals between python 2 & 3. One of the attributes I'm trying to be careful about is whether it's a "drop-in replacement", and this move was the only thing keeping `six.moves.urllib.parse` from being a drop-in replacement for `urlparse`. In other words, adding this means you can say "replace all imports of `urlparse` with `six.moves.urllib.parse` and it will _work_." 

Also, while I agree it's undocumented in Python, it'll also be undocumented in `six`, so it's not exactly "enshrining" it. 

In any case, please feel free to close this PR if you don't find these arguments convincing. Thanks again for writing this library!

@Ptr314, I fixed up the property error. To explain how it works, many objects returned by the Zendesk API contain ids that link related objects. Zenpy resolves these lazily when requested, and it does this via properties on the Zenpy objects. For example, when you execute the following:

```python
ticket = zenpy_client.tickets(id=5396)
print(ticket.requester)
```

The `ticket.requester` access is actually a property: https://github.com/facetoe/zenpy/blob/master/zenpy/lib/api_objects/__init__.py#L3595

Which calls this method in `Api`: https://github.com/facetoe/zenpy/blob/master/zenpy/lib/api.py#L310

Now, depending on whether or not the User is cached, it will either retrieve it from the cache or execute an API call to Zendesk to retrieve it.

So the error you were seeing in the test is because the `_get_default_locale` was not yet implemented in the `Api` class. I added it in, and now it's all good: https://github.com/facetoe/zenpy/commit/2149baa7ccf4d82f3f65f590f791919575280456#diff-b8941e80c13585f2d92cacceaee1d6eeR402.

A few comments about the previous commit 

1. I didn't update `plot_decision_region_slices` at all. That still needs to be done. 

2. This new version of `plot_decision_regions` should behave exactly the same as the previous version when there are 1 or 2 features (so no existing code will break).

3. For the case `X.shape[1] > 2`, the feature indices to be on the x and y axis are specified with `feature_index` (default is `(0, 1)`). And the filler values for the other features are provided by `filler_feature_dict` (default is `None`, but will raise a `ValueError` is not provided). For example, if you have a dataset with 3 training features and want to have the first and third features on the x and y axis, and the value `999` for the second feature, you would do something like 

```python
plot_decision_regions(X, y, clf, feature_index=(0, 2), filler_feature_dict={1: 999})
```
Passing the filler values in with a dictionary of index-value pairs is a little awkward, but I haven't been able to think of simpler way of doing it. Any suggestions? 

4. The main problem I ran into has to do with scatter plotting the training data. The problem arises because (I think) you really only want to plot the training data that has values for the filled feature near the filled values. For example, with the previous example, if the second feature is filled with the value `999`, then you probably only want to show training events that have a value for the second feature close to 999. Presumably, the value of the second feature will affect what the decision boundaries look like. 

My solution to this problem was...the null solution. I put `if dim <= 2:` before the scatter section of the code. Any thought on if the training data should be built into `plot_decision_regions` for `X.shape[1] > 2`?

Wait, sorry, I thought this was related to the above comments. The file you link to is a GFF, so there is no gene or transcript inference by default. It appears the issue with this file is the duplicated IDs.

Databases need a unique ID for each feature. The `ID` field in this file is not unique -- for example here are the features for `ID=cds1`:

```
NC_000001.11	Gnomon	CDS	182709	182746	.	+	0	ID=cds1;Parent=rna37;Dbxref=GeneID:102725121,Genbank:XP_011542110.1;Name=XP_011542110.1;gbkey=CDS;gene=LOC102725121;product=uncharacterized protein LOC102725121 isoform X1;protein_id=XP_011542110.1
NC_000001.11	Gnomon	CDS	183114	183240	.	+	1	ID=cds1;Parent=rna37;Dbxref=GeneID:102725121,Genbank:XP_011542110.1;Name=XP_011542110.1;gbkey=CDS;gene=LOC102725121;product=uncharacterized protein LOC102725121 isoform X1;protein_id=XP_011542110.1
NC_000001.11	Gnomon	CDS	183922	184158	.	+	0	ID=cds1;Parent=rna37;Dbxref=GeneID:102725121,Genbank:XP_011542110.1;Name=XP_011542110.1;gbkey=CDS;gene=LOC102725121;product=uncharacterized protein LOC102725121 isoform X1;protein_id=XP_011542110.1
```

If you use `merge_strategy="merge"`, then gffutils assumes the lines refer to the same feature and so does a lot of work to merge the attributes in a nice way. Looking at this file though, the CDSs should definitely be considered different features.

You'll need to decide how you want to be able to refer to CDSs. If you don't really have an opinion on that, you can try the `merge_strategy="create_unique"` argument when creating the database which should speed things up considerably. The features above will then be called `cds1`, `cds1.1`, and `cds1.2`. Alternatively you can write a transform function to do arbitrary manipulation of the features before they get into the db, for example to create your own custom ID field based on the other attributes.

Give `merge_strategy="create_unique"` a try to see if it helps the speed issue. It still should run in <15 mins.

I think you should do the scaling inside `convert_to_numpy`.  That's a much simpler way to code it.  I also don't want to enshrine a particular normalization strategy in a pixel type, especially since this one is peculiar to some kinds of neural networks and not generally what people doing image processing on images that happen to have multiple floating point bands want.  

I would be open to adding a multi-band float pixel type that behaved the way the existing single band float pixel types do, which is to say no scaling. They simply contain whatever the underlying value was and when being converted to other pixel types they perform a simple saturation at the max or min values of the target type. 

Also, the code you posted is a little more complex and error prone than it needs to be.  There is manual memory management which should not be there.  It also is destructive of the source image array when it doesn't need to be since it's not like you pass ownership of the source image memory blocks to python, they are simply copied and then destroyed.  You shouldn't be stealing the memory block at all here since you just immediately destroy it. In general you should try and not have raw owning pointers since it creates the opportunity for a memory leak.

Also don't call malloc. Instead you should just make a py::array_t of the appropriate size using the py::array_t's constructor.  Let it allocate it's own memory.  Then just use a for loop to copy the data into the py::array_t you made.

@XhmikosR Sorry for the ignorance before, I was away from GitHub for some days.

First of all thank you for your kindness to help, I will explain this problem to you as clearly as possible :)

There are two things we should know before understanding why this happens:

1. People in the world use different [Decimal Mark](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Decimal_mark#Hindu.E2.80.93Arabic_numeral_system) in different countries, mainly in two format: `.` (e.g. `0.003`) and `,` (e.g. `0,003`).

2. httpstat tells curl to output statistics like this

   ```
   curl -w '{"time_connect": %{time_connect}}' google.com
   ```

   and decode the result as json for further use in the program.

For people in countries using point as decimal mark, like US or China, it will output `{"time_connect": 0.069}` which is a valid json. But for people using comma as decimal mark, it will output `{"time_connect": 0,069}`, which is not a valid json, thus if we use Python to decode it directly, the exception will happen.

```
$ python -c "import json; json.loads('{\"time_connect\": 0,069}')"
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.12/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/__init__.py", line 339, in loads
    return _default_decoder.decode(s)

To avoid this, httpstat calls `curl` with a environment set: `LC_ALL=C`, in Unix system, it means to let applications to use default language for output (more on [stackoverflow](http://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/87745/what-does-lc-all-c-do)), thus `curl` will use pointer decimal mark for number even your computer's localization setting uses comma decimal mark.

But when it comes to Windows, setting `LC_ALL=C` does not work at all, `curl` still follows system's localization, and eventually this problem happens.

From my perspective, I want to know **whether application's language settings could be changed during runtime in Windows**, no matter it's through environment variables or not, should there be a way, I can make httpstat do the same thing for `curl`. That's why I tried `set LANG=C` before, I also tried searching around for that, but still don't have a clue.

And lastly, if there's nothing like `LC_ALL=C` in Windows, there's still a way to solve this problem: change the output to `{"time_connect": "0,069"}` so that the number is treated as string, then I can replace `,` to `.` and convert the string back to number. This is tricky and not elegant, only to be a last resort.

value = packet.wlan_mgt.supported_rates

For use cases where it's important to parse Python code (a) across different Python versions and (b) quickly, the [typed_ast](https://github.com/dropbox/typed_ast) project may be a good choice. This is work that @ddfisher did quite recently -- it's new since this thread started -- for [Mypy](https://github.com/python/mypy), which is a typechecker for PEP 484 static types.

The standard library's `ast` module is pretty great in some important ways:
* It's very fast, because it's a thin wrapper around CPython's own parser.
* It produces a nice AST that reflects the structure of Python code well.

The trouble with it from our perspective was twofold:
* Because it's a thin wrapper around the Python interpreter's own parser, the syntax you parse is the syntax of the Python version your program is itself running under -- so a Python 3 program can't use `ast` to parse Python 2 code.
* We need to parse [PEP 484 type annotations](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0484/), some of which appear in comments, which the Python interpreter and `ast` naturally discard.

So we borrowed the CPython parser -- forked it, effectively -- and fixed those issues:
* We modified the CPython parser slightly to make an extension module you can import independently of the version of Python your own program using the parser is running on, and did so for both CPython 2.7's and CPython 3.5's parsers so that you can parse either Python 2 or Python 3 code.
* We added full support for PEP 484 type annotations.

Because Python's syntax is quite stable and so is CPython's parser -- a new version every year or two, generally with modest changes -- we're quite comfortable with maintaining this "fork" as Python 3.6, 3.7, and so on come out with new syntactical features.

At present `typed_ast` will not be helpful for code-transformation tools that need to be able to render the source code back out losslessly. I think that may not actually be hard to fix, though, and we may even do so ourselves just for the sake of clear error messages in Mypy: if you track the byte offsets in the file at which a given node starts and ends (just like the CPython parser already tracks the line and column numbers at which it starts), you can always read the exact form of any node or of the whitespace between nodes out of a single string containing the original file.

It's unlikely `typed_ast` will ever handle proceeding past a syntax error. It'll also never be pure Python, though because the interface is essentially the same as the stdlib's `ast`, someone else may already have a pure-Python implementation of that interface.

Although `typed_ast` was originally created for Mypy's needs, we broke it out separately precisely because we think it may be useful for other tools and are happy to share. If it seems like a potential fit for the use cases of anyone here, please try it out -- we'd be glad to hear from you with issues and/or pull requests.

Hi Paul, assuming that the request you're doing is "GET http://somewhere/foo", and that it's coming from a script that was served from http://127.0.0.1:4220, then ensure that the response coming from http://somewhere/foo satisfies the following criteria:
1. Either:
1a. Has a header "Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET"
or
1b. Has a header "Access-Control-Allow-Methods: *"
and
2. Either:
2a. Has a header "Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://127.0.0.1:4220"
or
2b. Has a header "Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *"
and
3. If the request has any headers that are not in the list of 'safe' headers, then the response must have a header "Access-Control-Allow-Headers:", with value either all of the headers that weren't in that 'safe' list, or "*".
The list of 'safe' headers is in the CORS specification at: http://www.w3.org/TR/cors/

Thanks for letting me know.  This is apparently an [issue](https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/2732) with interactive setup.py files.  Recent versions of pip now suppresses all output from setup.py, so there is no way to tell that it might be waiting for a response from you.  Kind of annoying, but they seem to think this is a feature, not a bug, so I guess it's on me to change setup.py to deal with this.

Here is the message that is apparently being suppressed:
```
WARNING: TreeCorr uses cffi, which in turn requires libffi to be installed.
         As the latter is not a python package, pip cannot download and
         install it.  However, it is fairly straightforward to install.

On Linux, you can use one of the following:

    apt-get install libffi-dev
    yum install libffi-devel

On a Mac, it should be available after you do:

    xcode-select --install

If neither of those work for you, you can install it yourself with the
following commands:

    wget ftp://sourceware.org:/pub/libffi/libffi-3.2.1.tar.gz
    tar xfz libffi-3.2.1.tar.gz
    cd libffi-3.2.1
    ./configure --prefix={prefix}
    make
    make install
    cp */include/ffi*.h {prefix}/include
    cd ..

If you have already done this, then check the command (given above) that failed.  You may
need to add a directory to either C_INCLUDE_PATH, LIBRARY_PATH, or LD_LIBRARY_PATH to
make it succeed.

Stop the installation here to take care of this? [Y/n] 
```
(Of course, it will format this so {prefix} is the right thing given where you are trying to install TreeCorr.)

Clearly, I should have this query time-out after some reasonable length of time, so it doesn't hang.  And I'll look to see if there is a way to tell that it is being run by pip, so the user can't even see the question, in which case I can just have it fail immediately.  Although I'm not sure how to get the message about why it's failing past the pip firewall.  I'll need to investigate that.

In any case, the immediate solution for you is probably to just make sure libffi is installed (as described above) and try again.  You could also try installing from source where you will see the output of what setup.py is trying to do in case there are any other issues.

Ok here it is:
* you can remove the `(0 - 800: white, 800-900: yellow, >900 - red)` which is meant as a comment for the coloring variable.
* you can remove the `name` parameter because you already use an instance name `vnstat down`
* you should use the `format` parameter to say what you want to display for each instance, by default it's the `{total}` that's displayed which is why you have 3 times the same output

For example, for the `down` instance you would get:
````
    vnstat down {
        format = "down: {down}"
        coloring = {
            800: "#dddd00",
            900: "#dd0000",
         }
    }
````

The `format` parameter is cool because you have total freedom about what you display. To see the available placeholders (`{variables}`) see the doc by issuing `py3status modules details` !

Hi !

Big changes following the nice recommandations I got in feedback:

- `cache_timeout` is now automatically set depending on what's in the `format` string: if the smallest unit you use is `{minutes}`, it will be cached for 60 seconds. But if the smallest you use is `{days}`, then the cache timeout will be 86400 seconds.
- In the same way, not every unit is computed, only the ones you need! It allows you to have the same behavior than the `uptime` command: if you don't use the `{year}` placeholder, you will have uptimes with, for example, 542 days. This behavior is documented in the module's docstring.

However, if you or other users want to have your own CLI program that uses FFF to do whatever, that's fine.  And I'm willing to provide new FFF features to help with it:

- There's already a `post_process_cmd` option available in personal.ini that could be used for adding/updating via `calibredb`.  I'm willing to incorporate additional hooks if they can be sufficiently isolated.

The plugin version has an option like that: 'Overwrite if Newer', and in CLI, there's an INI option `always_overwrite` which defaults to false (and gives the warning you saw).
```
## Always overwrite output files.  Otherwise, the downloader checks
## the timestamp on the existing file and only overwrites if the story
## has been updated more recently.  Command line version only
#always_overwrite: true
```
The downside is most sites only give an updated _date_, not time.  So FFF only stops the overwrite if the file date is _greater than_ the updated date.  If you run it more than once on the day the update comes out, it will run the full download and overwrite more than once.

Epub updates (-u/--update-epub) causes chapter count to take precedence over the file date vs updated date.

If updated date is more recent than file date while chapter count is unchanged may indicate changes, but we've no way of knowing where (which chapters).  You can manually run download again without update in that case.

None of which helps with the original request, which was about skipping super-short chapters on the theory they were author blather instead of content.

We try to be smart about rate limiting, but sometimes twitter will provide the wrong rate limit data depending on the edge server you’re hitting. There have some other issues on this topic if you want to check it out. 

I see that you’re running this on a cron script, so one possibility is that, if one run of the script hasn’t completed and a new one is started, you’ll hit the rate limit on the first run of the second running instance. 

You can wrap the GetFollowers call in a try/except block and check if `88` is in the error. In terms of your use case, I’d suggest using GetUser, which will return the number of followers as part of the User object returned, otherwise you’re going to be hitting the rate limit incredibly quickly, which may be the cause of the issue in the second paragraph above.

I think we should make this an option for the _configure_ method by taking into account the following:
* there is no dedicated data type for boolean 
* CANape and Inca treat single bit unsigned channels as logical channels (see also issue #53)
* there is added information to single bit channels that is lost if they are treated as uint8 channels (this happens with _cut_, _convert_, _filter_, _concatenate_ and _stack_ methods)
* everybody can choose how single bit unsigned values are treated (bool/uint8)

The best way would probably be to pass the new configuration option to _get_fmt_v3_, _get_fmt_v4_, _fmt_to_datatype_v3_ and _fmt_to_datatype_v4_

To be honest, I am not sure how I would go about this. As the two snippets of text do not share much structure. You can make the parser very generic, such as this:

```
words = word (_ word)+
word = ~"\w+"
_ = ~"\s+"
```

That will just give you a list of words though, so there is not much benefit to actually parsing here compared to just `re.finditer(r'\w+', that_text_you_want_parsed)`, except for the parse nodes also matching the whitespace.

Taking a step back from the concrete examples: It _kind_ of looks like you want to do natural language processing, is that the case? I don't think PEGs are very well suited for that, but I could totally be mistaken, as I don't know much about parse theory. Could be that there is some trick I just don't know about :)

I guess it depends what model of cache you're thinking about. The single file cache is easy for the application to build without requiring different tools to co-ordinate. But cache invalidation is really hard that way - if I install a plugin systemwide with `yum` or `apt`, it can't update the cache for every user who might have used that application. You could do it manually, with a 'check for new plugins' button/command, but that loses the plug-and-play benefit of entry points. Applications can do this kind of caching without any changes to the entry points system: get their entry points once and save the results.

With the per-directory model, cache invalidation still isn't easy, but if you can get all the tools that (un)install packages to run some hooks, applications should mostly be able to rely on the cache, with manual intervention only rarely needed. I'm basing this idea on the XDG mime system: mime types are declared in a collection of separately installable XML files, and installer code runs `update-mime-database` after changing those files to rebuild a database, which is what applications read.

I don't think files being on NFS is a particular problem unless we use SQLite (and that's why I'm not using it). Loading them will be slower, but so will loading any modules and data files.

> Seeing as the scope of dataclasses still seems fairly pliable, I'll share an answer to #19 

I would put that differently. In my view, an important property that dataclasses should have is being easily extendable. They should provide a common way of adding functionalities that would otherwise be added via some boilerplate, for example, the serialization code. Importantly, such extensions should be easily _composable_ (i.e. should not interfere with each other). Since Python 3.6 we have several additional ways of customizing classes (such as ``__init_subclass__``) that allow avoiding metaclass conflicts.

We don't need to include all functionality in stdlib (although some serialization support might be OK), but it should be possible to do something like this:
```python
from third_party import data_serializer

@data(serializer=data_serializer)
class C:
    x: List[int] = field(factory=list)
```
I am still not sure how exactly to organize such composable extensions, it looks we need to preserve the original namespace (that contains ``field(...)`` values) so that it is accessible by the extensions.

I think @ncoghlan's major point here is that we should have arguments that describe how the class will be used, not how it will be implemented.

But, I don't see a lot of precedence for this in core Python types, so I think we should stick with the attrs-like description of what features you're asking for in the generated class, not how you plan to use it. For example, if you want to add your own `__repr__`, you'd set `repr=False`. There's a direct correspondence with parameter names and Python features. I don't think hiding Python features serves the users.

For example, what would `slots=False` become? You'd need some parameter or combination of parameters that means you want to have instances where you're able to add dynamic attributes that are non-fields, and you're okay with the increased per-instance memory usage.

I'm still trying to get to the bottom of this issue, but it seems there is an archive.org bug where our servers hang up the phone before sending the full response to the client. The library I use for HTTP requests, `requests`, doesn't seem to have a way to turn off connection pooling. This seems to cause an issue when archive.org hangs up the phone early, and the connection gets returned to the pool in a borked state.

There is a change in v1.7.7 that I was hoping would help get around this issue, until the bug can be addressed on archive.org. I added a `Connection:close` header to each request, to make sure the connection gets closed before being returned to the pool.

Cool.  This raises a great point.  I guess the reason CamelCase "felt right" is that I'm thinking about them like subclasses, but you're right -- that's not what they *are*.

I think you're not alone with UPPERCASE, because after all, we're defining constants (or at least pseudo-constants), and that is consistent with PEP 8.

Thank you for digging into Django as a point of reference.  Based on this I was curious to dig deeper into Python.  Warning: long post ahead.

---

## PEP 435

Particularly, after confirming there is no guideline for this in PEP 8, I started with *PEP 0435 -- Adding an Enum type to the Python standard library*.  The convention used throughout the document is snake_case, though it is never explicitly called out or prescribed.  So then I wondered: Where did it come from?

Also, the [discussion mentions](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0435/#status-of-discussions) a previously rejected PEP for enums and also Guido's proposal to just add `flufl.enum` to stdlib.  Okay, let's look there.

## `flufl.enum`

In [its examples](http://fluflenum.readthedocs.org/en/latest/docs/using.html#class-syntax), `flufl.enum` uses snake_case.  I still found this odd because we define an enum as a collection of related constants.  The documentation [is consistent with this definition](http://fluflenum.readthedocs.org/en/latest/docs/using.html?highlight=constant) even if the naming convention is not:

> An enumeration is a set of symbolic names bound to unique, constant values, called enumeration items.

and

> The properties of an enumeration are useful for defining an immutable, related set of constant values that have a defined sequence but no inherent semantic meaning.

So if they're constants, we should name them `LIKE_A_CONSTANT`, right?

The motivation for flufl.enum mentions that it is lifted from PEP 354, the original (rejected) PEP for enums.

## PEP 354

So I dug into *PEP 0354 -- Enumerations in Python*.  It also uses snake_case, but digging deeper there's [a note that it was inspired by something else](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0354/#implementation):

> This design is based partly on a recipe [2] from the Python Cookbook.

Where [2] is [First Class Enums in Python](http://aspn.activestate.com/ASPN/Cookbook/Python/Recipe/413486) written by Zoran Isailovski in 2005, using an abbreviated CamelCase convention.

## Earlier

Zoran's snippet uses this style:

```
...
Days = Enum('Mo', 'Tu', 'We', 'Th', 'Fr', 'Sa', 'Su')
print Days
print Days.Mo
...
```

That implementation also makes reference to two earlier implementations: One by [Will Ware in 2001](http://code.activestate.com/recipes/67107/) using UPPERCASE; the other by [Samuel Reynolds in 2004](http://code.activestate.com/recipes/305271/) using CamelCase.

(At this point I tell myself we've come full circle.)

## `enum` itself

Finally, I decide we'll look back to `enum` in the Python 3 docs, but even it uses multiple styles.

Ex. [8.13.2. Creating an Enum](https://docs.python.org/3.4/library/enum.html#creating-an-enum)

```
from enum import Enum
class Color(Enum):
    red = 1
    green = 2
    blue = 3
```

Ex. [8.13.13.4. Planet](https://docs.python.org/3.4/library/enum.html#planet)

```
class Planet(Enum):
    MERCURY = (3.303e+23, 2.4397e6)
    VENUS   = (4.869e+24, 6.0518e6)
    ...
```

## Conclusion

After all this, I'm somewhat split between snake_case and UPPERCASE.

I think UPPERCASE better represents constant-ness, but for a reason I wasn't able to find, the community at some point picked up using snake_case for naming constants defined inside enums.

Of course, as you've shown Django chooses to use UPPERCASE in their own examples.  And since this is a Django-specific library, I feel inclined to follow their convention, especially having explicitly documented rationale.

Perhaps it's just that the greater Python community hasn't questioned the enum naming convention in detail.

I'm not sure whether this changes your conclusion, but at least I feel more informed.

Okay, there are some pretty interesting corner cases here.  Let's say I have the following:

```python
spec = {
  'my_string': {'type': 'str', 'fallback': 'fallback_str'},
  'fallback_str': {'type': 'str', 'default': 'default_from_fallback'},
}
```

If we load the config, the following should work:

```python
config = spec.load_config()
assert config == {'my_string': 'default_from_fallback', 'fallback_str': 'default_from_fallback'}
```

Now let's say we had a config like the following:

```python
config = spec.load_config(
  {'fallback_str': 'config1_value'}, 
  {'my_string': 'config2_value'}
)
```

Should `my_string == "config1_value"` or should `my_string == "config2_value"`?

```python
my_folder = Folder(name'My Folder', parent=my_account.inbox)
my_folder.save()
another_folder = Folder(name'Another Folder', parent=my_folder)
another_folder.save()
```

There is no such thing as a "multi-byte string" in Python terminology, so I don't know what you mean. There are only:

* unicode strings (series of Unicode code points). This is what Django and django-paypal use internally, and what it sends to the database.
* byte strings (series of bytes, usually obtained by taking a unicode string and encoding using a specified encoding e.g. UTF-8). Byte strings are needed for communicating over HTTP, because the internet only knows about bytes.

django-paypal is designed to work with unicode strings internally, and will correctly decode byte strings received from PayPal.  It is possible it has bugs when creating byte strings to PayPal - I'll need to check.

```python
from flask_restplus import Api as rpapi

@property
def specs_url(self):
    """Fixes issue where swagger-ui makes a call to swagger.json over HTTP.
       This can ONLY be used on servers that actually use HTTPS.  On servers that use HTTP,
       this code should not be used at all.
    """
    return flask.url_for(self.endpoint('specs'), _external=True, _scheme='https')
```

Hello, Dave. Currently it's not possible to do that. On issue #24 we discuss the possibility of logging both the full updated value, and in another field, only the values that changed since the last update. But we have no plans to log what the value was before the update.

If you want to do that, you need to tweak some things. If you know about signals, you should change the file "signals.py", and make it use the signal `pre_save` instead of the current `post_save`. That way, before saving you could access the original object with a query, and then compare that object with the instance being saved. Check out [this SO thread](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/5582410/django-how-to-access-original-unmodified-instance-in-post-save-signal).

Hope it helps. Thank you!

The `Invalid <section> padding`and  `invalid header string` are errors that occur before the secret is used to try and validate the token. The first ones mean that you have altered the  JWT in such a way that one of the [sections](https://scotch.io/tutorials/the-anatomy-of-a-json-web-token#toc-what-does-a-jwt-look-like) no longer contains valid base64 and cannot be base64 decoded. The second one means that after  base64 decoding the segment, it is not a valid utf-8 string. 

These will not pose security issues like what you linked above, as these can all be verified and have the same results shown locally by anyone who has the invalid token. The only error in your list that actually tries to validate the token using the secret key is the `Signature verification failed` error.

That said, I'm not really opposed to returning a uniformed error message in all cases. I don't think it would change very much in practice though. If you wanted to submit a PR for that, I could look over it.

Cheers.

Well, an asynchronous application needs to be fully asynchronous. Even if you have one function that is blocking, that breaks the whole thing.

Gevent allows you to monkey patch the Python standard library and make many of its blocking functions non-blocking. This helps in many cases. If this external function that you are using does any networking or threading, the monkey patching will allow those to be done in a cooperative way. If this function is just using CPU, then I'm afraid it is a problem. You will need to move that function to another thread or process, so that it does not interfere with the asynchronous tasks. Maybe the `gevent.threadpool` module can help with that.

```python

```

This should look like

```python
router.register(r'foo', FooViewSet, base_name='foo'
```

Coverage remained the same at 60.709% when pulling **01b79a16edbddef951b4a69ad21a6f227e950d58 on pmatos:patch-2** into **a40e6a5400cc29550c660541181748182f83ba8e on SoCo:master**.

**URI prefix research for comment** (hard to get all the details - but this should be enough!)

It appears Sonos has four display modes:
     1) Line In              Controls shown: none - no stop, pause, play, or seek
     2) TV In                 Controls shown: none - no stop, pause, play, or seek
     3) Track                 Controls shown: pause, play, seek         Display: Song / Artist/ Album
     4) Radio/Stream   Controls shown: stop, play - no seek     Display: Station / On Now / Information

In SoCo we have functions:
-   switch_to_line_in  and switch_to_tv for 1 and 2. 
-   Track 3 can be set by using play_from_queue.
-   Track 3 and Radio / Stream 4 are both set using play_uri.
	
So for play_uri we need to have the optional ability to say if the uri should be
played as a stream (radio) or as a track.
From testing the following prefixes (URI schemes) deafult to:

	default to Radio / Stream mode:
		x-sonosapi-stream:
		x-rincon-mp3radio:
		hls-radio:
		
	default to Track mode:
		aac:
		http:
		https:
		x-file-cifs:
		
The following also exist for radio but can't find a working example with Sonos: 	mms:  rtsp:
	
Sonos also has a range of prefixes for Tracks specific to each service eg:
	x-sonos-spotify:          (Spotify)
	x-sonosapi-hls-static:  (Amazon Prime)
	x-sonos-http:		    (Google Play & Napster)
	
I belive only prefixes that can be either track or stream are:
		aac:
		http:
		https:
	so we need to give the user the option to force these to Stream / Radio mode.
	This can simply be done by substiuting the prefix with 'x-rincon-mp3radio:'
        However 'hls-radio:' also benefits from using the 'x-rincon-mp3radio:' prefix.

	**def play_uri(self, uri='', meta='', title='', start=True, force-stream=False):**
         If True any prefix is substituted with 'x-rincon-mp3radio:' else the supplied uri is used as is.

Alternatively to make it more future proof we could have the parameter called 
        play_as='', or play_as='radio'

import soco

uri = [

    'hls-radio://a.files.bbci.co.uk/media/live/manifesto/audio/simulcast/hls/uk/sbr_high/llnw/bbc_6music.m3u8',

def play_it(uri):

for u in uri:

there are *many* initialization problems in angr. We basically have to implement our own copy of the linux loader, and there are many, many ways this can go wrong.

We don't have the time to investigate this specific issue for you right now, so here are some tips for tackling it on your own:

- You've already found STRICT_PAGE_ACCESS, this is good
- ErroredPath.debug() will get you a debug shell at the crash site. Use this to figure out where you're trying to write, and where this value came from
- Follow along the trace (path.trace.hardcopy) in IDA or another disassembler and see if there's any obvious point you go wrong
- Essentially, try to find the very first thing that's gone wrong, and try to track it back to "here's a thing that the loader should have done but didn't"

From the error it looks like a worker has died a few times while trying to read data from S3.  My first guess would be some sort of mismatched environment issue.  I recommend the following, in order of difficulty:

1.  Restarting your cluster with `client.restart()`
2.  Verifying that all of your workers have the same software environment with `client.get_versions(check=True)` (although this may not check the recent update to dask-ml master)
3.  Verifying manually that you have installed the same software everywhere

The following patch works for me:

Given the quorum needs for etcd and ceph, and the growing number of other CAPish things that also need quorum (zookeeper, mesos-master, influxdb...), it seems best to break off a static control plane of 3-9 nodes that services these needs.

Any auto-scaling-group should be on fleet tagged application tiers with no quorum requirement, so that applications can be scaled dynamically to thousands of nodes if need be, and there can be less concern about retaining quorum.

This is something I'm working on here presently. CloudFormations can be nested with `AWS::CloudFormation::Stack`.

It would be ideal to have a top-level primary _VPC stack_ with subnet, ELB, and other related "glue" that all of the nested stacks must rely on.

Under that _VPC stack_, a _control stack_ would be a statically defined as a collection of `AWS::EC2::Instance` resources with `DisableApiTermination` set to `true`. These CoreOS instances would act as the etcd election leadership, as well as the ceph monitor quorum, and other other CAP quorum engines necessary for keeping a highly available replicated state.

Under that _control stack_ would be one or more fleet tagged _application stacks_. These stacks would be built on `AWS::AutoScaling::AutoScalingGroup` resources that have no inherent state and can grow and shrink without consequence.

Eventually, multiple other _service stacks_ could also be managed under the _VPC stack_ without directly impeding the function of the _control stack_.

This is the direction we are headed. I would love to contribute this back to the deis project, though I'm concerned that this is CloudFormation (AWS/Eucalyptus) centric - which may be importable to HEAT (OpenStack), but definitely isn't cloud agnostic like TerraForm.

The environment variable does not preclude the option flag, and the environment variable is only needed when a flag is not viable (i.e. for the “courtesy notice”, and `pipenv run` etc.). From the user’s perspective, verbose mode is still activated with `--verbose` and `-v` as before, only the internal representation of this changed, so we can adapt to a more elaborated verbosity switch mechanism.

Cargo is actually a fine example here—it has a normal mode, `-q`, `-v`, and `-vv`. Internally this is represented a an enum, just like it is represented as an integer here (because Python does not have a language-native enum).

I would agree maybe the user should be able to use boolean-based environment variables to control the internal value though. I’ll add a couple of extra entries for that.

> Can you verify whether mounting the host’s cache directory into Docker works (using -v; I’m not sure what the correct term is here)?

Bind mounts. It would (probably) work, but actually there's other issues with that. Namely, the docker service runs as root (meaning it writes files as root), and the container itself runs root as well (as is common). Pip will refuse to use a cache from a mismatching UID, so mounting ~1000/.cache/pip will do nothing unless the container itself has a UID 1000, and even if it does, it will write files as root which is not something you want.

Having a hash in `Pipfile.lock` will likely make it painful to work with git (if committed). 

Example 1:

- I add flask to my `Pipfile` and create a `Pipfile.lock` (works)
- I create a feature branch and add `sqlalchemy` (works)
- I add my `requests` to master (works)
- I merge my `sqlalchemy` branch (merge conflict, sha mismatch)

Example 2:

- I'm working on a bigger migration from `celery 3` to `celery 4` in a feature branch.
- In the meantime `Django` issues a new security release and I'm updating my `Pipfile` and `Pipfile.lock`
- celery 4 is finally ready to be merged, I'm getting `Pipfile.lock` merge conflicts

Example 3:

- I'm using automated tools to update my dependencies in their own branches, one at a time
- I'm getting merge conflicts all over the place as soon as more than one update is available

The only way around that would be to tell people to never ever commit their `Pipfile.locks`, or to remove the hash.

As of now, here's what I see in the contenders for the Pipfile format:

```
DSL
  + Python-esque
  + Looks good and neat for nearly all usecases
  + Familiar syntax to everyone expected to use this file
  + Good support for optional arguments
  - Will require effort to support from tools
    . can be supported by pure-Python tools easily - use same library as pip
  - Involves a "new" learning curve (kinda)
  - Differences between various Python versions
    - Would need own separate parser implementation.

TOML
  + Already a part of future ecosystem - PEP518 starts the process
  + Has a (really nice) specification
  + Used by Rust community for their packaging needs
  + Has existing packages in various languages
  - Can get non Python-esque (and ugly) in complex cases

TOML with an optional executable Python Pipfile.in
  Everything in TOML
  + Easier for programically generating the final Pipfile
  - Another extra file; can add confusion.
```

Every other format has been eliminated because of the same reasons as they were in PEP-518.

------

If I could do whatever I wanted, I would yank out a spec for something like YAML and ask *everyone* to just use it where it makes sense in their domain.

More realistically though, as @dstufft puts it:

> So far every attempt I've seen at this comes down to a single trade off, Ease of Implementation for tool authors versus ease of use for end users.

## TOML

To me, the trade-offs seems most "balanced" with TOML. I just like how the picture comes together with it. There's a `pyproject.toml` and a `Pipfile`. Both use the same format. Files of that format can be easily generated by some tool from some other syntax that the end-user fancies, because it's a standard format.

## DSL

As I weighed up all of these options, I realized that the Python DSL is actually really nice. But there's just some really bad issues with it:

- It's a DSL

  It's a custom language. Tools will have to be written to support it. This can  be largely handled if the support library pip would use is designed with support for editing the file in mind. That, it so happens, is also a valid use-case to support even for pip as `--save` is something we want.

  Even with this support library, this DSL would restricted to merely Python-only tools. If it's deemed worth it, then we could add some standardized Intermediate Structure that the support library would produce non-Python tools to touch it. It'll take effort but it can be done.

- Python is a moving target

  The thing is, Python, the language itself, is constantly evolving. The most recent major release 3.6.0 saw the addition of multiple new syntax features. If the DSL implementation uses the AST, these features are not going to be available on older versions and the file won't the parsable on them if it uses these files.

  This means that if the DSL needs to be consistent across Python versions, it would have to be implemented in a manner independent of the Python version used - not using the stdlib provided AST but a dedicated parser.

  A dedicated parser is a lot of work and while being the Python-like, the DSL would be a completely independent from Python - the language.

At which point, this leads to the following decisions to be made:
- How important is the consistency across Python versions?
- If it's important, does the effort on maintaining a custom declarative DSL have justified rewards in usability?

The answers to these questions lead me to lean toward TOML.

Honestly though, if we settle on anything other than TOML, I think it's worth discussion (on `distutils-sig`) if we should then switch syntax for `pyproject.toml` to the same as well. (I wrote this assuming that YAML was still on the cards, applies to the DSL less so)

Just to restate an important thing, this issue doesn't need to hamper the ongoing development of a prototype. It just needs to be finalized before this makes it into pip.

In the threads I mentioned above, the "powers that be" were very insistent that wheels are not a full replacement of eggs, just of the installation aspect of eggs.

Many wheels can be added still zipped to the PYTHONPATH and their code will be available to Python (but not all, se below), but I'm not sure if `pkg_resources` would be able to read their metadata in this format.

However, a careful reading of the wheel spec shows that wheels that contain a directory like `wheel_name-1.0.data/platlib` or `wheel_name-1.0.data/purelib` are explicitly incompatible with being added to the `PYTHONPATH`, zipped or not. They would need to be unpacked and theses subdirectories moved to the root of the unpacking directory before this directory could be added to the `PYTHONPATH`.

At this point, the resulting directory looks pretty much like an unzipped egg, except for the `wheel_name-1.0.dist-info` directory instead of `EGG-INFO`, but it looks like `pkg_resources` can already handle this format of metadata as well.

Anyway, by following the way `.exe` files (created by `bdist_wininst`) are handled by `easy_install` and `package_index`, (including the directory mapping done by `get_exe_prefixes()` to handle `purelib` and `platlib` directories) one should be able to come up with a PR that will enable `setuptools` to handle `wheel` files that will benefit all users of `setuptools`.

The trace context propagation doesn't exist in that version yet, but does exist in our internal jaeger-client-python repo, which also supports instrumentation of HTTP call sites. However, in any case, the method of propagating the context is always going to be somewhat specific to a particular instrumentation library, and it's better not to monkey patch stuff if we can avoid it, so I would still recommend having a construction-time hook in TChannel where application can register a functor that will return current span, retrieved by whatever means are appropriate for the current instrumentation library. E.g. with Jaeger instrumentation, that function already exists:
```
def get_current_span():
    """
    :return: Returns current span associated with the current stack context, or None.
    """
    return TraceContextManager.current_span()

Looking again at your question, if you are looking to bind an existing static public IP to an existing VM, then you would do the following:

```
az network nic ip-config update -g {rg} --nic-name {nic_name} -n {ip_config_name} --public-ip-address {name or ID of your static public IP}
```

You can use `vm show` to get the name of the VM's NIC and `network nic show` to find out the IP configuration you want to change. Alternatively, you can list the NICs for your resource group and find the shows as associated with your VM (one call instead of two).

Running into the same issue here. This looks related to uuid.py from the python framework getting a 64 bit hardware address for the loopback adapter in High Sierra, specifically in _ifconfig_getnode(). The function expects a 48 bit mac address, but is instead getting 64 bits back and converting it to an int value that is too long for the subsequent call in uuid.py.

This is a sample output of the call to ifconfig that is being parsed:
```
b'lo0: flags=8049<UP,LOOPBACK,RUNNING,MULTICAST> mtu 16384\n'
b'\toptions=1203<RXCSUM,TXCSUM,TXSTATUS,SW_TIMESTAMP>\n'
b'\tinet 127.0.0.1 netmask 0xff000000 \n'
b'\tinet6 ::1 prefixlen 128 \n'
b'\tlladdr 70:cd:60:ff:ab:cd:ef:12 \n'
```

As you can see, the lladdr is longer than the usual 48 bit mac address. This issue should be addressed in the uuid.py module from the python project.

I've created an issue in bugs.python.org for this: https://bugs.python.org/issue32502

I am surprised and disappointed that we sort of seem to be bike-shedding about rather basic functionality of a CLI, and that apparently, there isn't a prescribed, documented, and tested way of implementing "ensure resource" logic for Azure with the official CLI.

To be specific:

1. The current behavior doesn't follow conventions of other CLI tools. Docker, Kubectl, Gcloud, AWS CLI, *none* of them behave this way. It would, in my opinion, be far more appropriate to return a non-zero exit code and a string message to standard error.

    Docker:
    ```
    $ docker stats thisdoesnotexist; echo $?
    Error response from daemon: No such container: thisdoesnotexist
    1
    ```

    AWS:
    ```
    $ aws ec2 describe-instance-status --instance-ids="i-0598c7d356eba48d7"; echo $?
    An error occurred (InvalidInstanceID.NotFound) when calling the DescribeInstanceStatus operation: The instance ID 'i-0598c7d356eba48d7' does not exist
    255
    ```

    GCP:
    ```
    $ gcloud compute instances describe notarealinstance --zone=us-west1-a; echo $?
    ERROR: (gcloud.compute.instances.describe) Could not fetch resource:
     - The resource 'projects/t2hrowawa2/zones/us-west1-a/instances/notarealinstance' was not found
    
    1
    ```

    Kubectl:
    ```
    $ k get pod notarealpod; echo $?
    Error from server (NotFound): pods "notarealpod" not found
    1
    ```

    And then Azure:
    ```
    $ az group show -n thisaintreal; echo $?
    0
    ```

    **None** of them behave the way the Azure CLI has been made to behave.

2. This **flat out broke scripts that I had written before this change was made**. I feel bad for anyone with real production deployments who thought they could upgrade the CLI and expect basic idempotent resource creation scripts to work consistently. (Maybe this was announced as a breaking change and I couldn't find it?)

3. (There's no Portal involved here anywhere....)

4. I don't know what `set -e` has to do with it. I have `set -e` in all of my bash scripts, including the ones authored when `az` behaved like other, similar, unix tools.

Some day, it would be nice to write this, and have the CLI just do the right things:

```
az group ensure -n group0 -l westus2
az network vnet ensure -n net0 -g group0
az network subnet ensure -n subnet0 -v net0 -g group0
...
```

In lieu of that, an `exists` subcommand for _all_ resource types would be sufficient.

Whatever the answer is, please, *please*, document it and write an integration test so that I don't have to revisit my most basic provisioning scripts in another year.

And yeah, I guess if you're going to keep the existing behavior, it ought to be consistently... blank... so that users don't accidentally assume they can rely on exit codes.

The code looks like it should work, and I trust that you've used it successfully (I can't really test this myself, but we'll take it despite the fact we can't test it).

Though I have a minor objection to naming this ComputeManagementService...

This is mainly because all of the existing compute operations are currently in ServiceManagementService.  Unlike the node.js sdk, we never ended up splitting into separate Compute, Network, Storage management service classes.  If we did, or intended to do so in the future, then ComputeManagementService would indeed be the correct class to include these operations.

So I would prefer to include the 3 new operations in the existing ServiceManagementService class.

If we have a ComputeManagementService class, users may think that this is where all the VM stuff is (especially if they are familiar with node.js or the newer ARM apis), where in fact it would just be these 3 image publishing operations that most users can't use since most are not image publishers.

Sorry, I'm not sure I really understand your confusion. My best guess is that you are referring to the parameters of the functions decorated with `@ex.captured`, `@ex.command` and `@ex.automain`. I refer to as [captured functions](http://sacred.readthedocs.io/en/latest/configuration.html#captured-functions). The "magic" here is that sacred uses dependency injection to automatically fill these parameters (by name) from the configuration if they are not explicitly passed. That is indeed a bit uncommon in python, but they are not global variables. In fact, the idea of this mechanism is to avoid having to choose between either a global configuration or manually passing around the configuration all the time.

The high-level design is that there is typically an experiment/ingredient object that is used to define all the configuration scopes, captured functions, and commands. That object is the prefix of the decorator calls, and the type of function to add (config, captured, command, main, ...) is the second part.
When executed the experiment object creates a run object that has the final config, and is responsible for running the main function, capturing the outputs, and calling the observers.

This mechanism is rather deeply ingrained in Sacred ATM. So I don't think there is a *good* way sidestepping it. If you really wanted to, you could use `@ex.main` instead of `@ex.automain` and do this at the bottom of the script:

``` python
@ex.main
def main(config):
    # do your thing using the config
    pass

if __name__ == '__main__':
    run = ex._create_run()
    run(run.config)
```

That way you would never have to use `@ex.capture` and you could just pass the config dictionary and its entries around yourself. But I don't really see much of an advantage there.

Sacred assumes that the base-directory of the experiment is the directory where the main experiment file is. Modules are only considered sources if they are in a subdirectory of that base-directory. 
So in your case sacred treats mymodule as an external dependency of which it cannot determine the version number. That's why you'll see a `'mymodule==<unknown>'` in the list of dependencies. 

The immediate fixes are either: move `myexperiment.py` one directory up, or manually add the relevant sources with `ex.add_source_file()`. 

Other than that, I think the current behavior is a reasonable default, but it might be nice to be able to specify a custom base-directory. So I'll keep that as a feature request. This should also be pretty easy to implement in case you want to give it a try.

Hi @iAmNaz,

As a matter of fact - I've just finished fixing this bug, the fix is coming up in a few hours 😀.

As for a workaround - there are two simple options:
1. When you're calling  `create()`  - pass in the param `validation_set=None`. This will train the model without a validation set.
2. Use your own validation set. If you've already saved aside a few of the training sessions - just pass them in as another SFrame, to the `validation_set` param.
For example:
```
train = tc.SFrame(sessions_1-200.csv)
valid = tc.Sframe(sessions_201-250.csv)
model = tc.activity_classifier.create(train, ... , validation_set=valid)
```
Make sure to pass in whole sessions (don't split the same session between test and valid) and have even distribution of the target labels (e.g. "walking", "running" etc) in both training and validation sets.

> Problem with designated config file is that it's not really encrypted .. And anyone with the access to the machine can access it .

That's not necessarily true. The files in `~/.aws/` are stored in a user's home directory, so they can be confidential to a user. If someone has `root`/sudo access to the machine, they can read other users' home directories, but now there's a more fundamental problem that `credstash` cannot fix for you. If segregation of access is important to your use of `credstash`, I recommend you give each user their own machine, or at least their own accounts on a shared machine and limited privileges so that they cannot read each others' files.

For more information, I recommend watching @alex-luminal's talk, "Managing Secrets at Scale": https://vimeo.com/147644113

@willhobbs I wrote a new cloud cover to irradiance algorithm that's based on the ineichen function and the turbidity file. It's basically this... 

```python
solpos = location.get_solarposition(cloud_cover.index)
cs = location.get_clearsky(cloud_cover.index, model='ineichen')
# offset and cloud cover in decimal units here
ghi = (offset + (1 - offset) * (1 - cloud_cover)) * ghi_clear
dni = disc(ghi, solpos['zenith'], cloud_cover.index)['dni']
dhi = ghi - dni * np.cos(np.radians(solpos['zenith']))
```

See the [updated documentation](https://forecasting.energy.arizona.edu/pvlib/pvlibfx/pvlib-python/docs/sphinx/build/html/forecasts.html#cloud-cover-and-radiation) for more.

Lot's of room for experimentation and likely improvements. I have no idea how the accuracy of the two methods ('clearsky_scaling' and 'liujordan') compares.

When you click the "add fit" button in the main frame of Demag GUI you will always get a fit with a default name, the same occurs if it is your first fit and you double click on the list. This is because there isn't a good way to pick out what name to use so it picks a default generally of the form "Fit #". 

If you want to rename a fit you have created click on the text potion of the fit drop down menu and overwrite the name then push enter to save it. This does require you to do it manually as you do your analysis however.

If you want to analyse the data more quickly, or edit interpretations in mass you should use the Demag GUI tool Interpretation Editor (IE). The IE can be found under the tools menu of Demag GUI and allows mass creation of fits, mass alteration of fits, mass deletion of fits, easy marking interpretations bad/good, and easy high level means interpretation.

This is usually the way I use Demag GUI to manage interpretations. Here's an example:

If you want to rename say all default named interpretations called "Fit 1" to "LowT" or "Mag" you would

- Open IE (hotkey: ctrl-e)
- Ensure your current level is the level over which you want to enact change (usually this is set to site, but if you want to say edit all interpretations in the study you will need to change it to study). It is the second drop down box on the top.
- Type "Fit 1" into the search bar
- type new name into the name box
- click and highlight using shift/control/command/or ctrl-a all interpretations
- click "apply changes to highlighted fits"

You can also change color, or bounds using the same method. Searching using the search bar just helps if you have a large dataset.

You can use the IE help menu to get in context help by clicking on "Usage and Tips" (hotkey: ctrl-h) then clicking on what you want more info on.
There is more about it here: https://earthref.org/PmagPy/cookbook/#x1-510005.1.1

@george-unbotify Retries are not possible without server-based ACK which is a separate issue altogether. Without ACK, there is *no possibility, even in theory* to know whether the socket is dead unless the router actively sends ICMP to indicate the connection has been broken or kernel detects it by other means. This is a fundamental limitation of the TCP protocol and has been there since 1974.

If the socket errors out, the reconnect is called automatically after it's cleared.

Reconnect on every send if necessary: https://github.com/fluent/fluent-logger-python/blob/master/fluent/sender.py#L186
Close and reset socket if any error occurred and was detected: https://github.com/fluent/fluent-logger-python/blob/master/fluent/sender.py#L158

You misunderstand, what I mean is that tests which only test the interfaces between the individual parser components could very easily become invalided by changes, especially those which fundamentally change parser state management such as supporting streamed execution (at least some of the other approaches) or concurrent execution. The current tests are guaranteed to be correct because they simulate the actual parsing. So, I mean yes, the current tests are pretty large and hard to read for edge cases, but that's because they're behaviour-based integration tests rather than unit tests. So, if I were to delete the existing tests and replace them with all the equivalent unit tests, then I think I could easily end up in a situation where I either have to delete and re-implement most of the unit tests or they fail to test real parser execution, which is why I went with this approach for testing to begin with aside from that I thought this was clearer since it's fairly obvious what user-facing behaviour each test is testing. --- *Comments from [Reviewable](https://reviewable.io:443/reviews/google/mobly/416)*

Global installation via `sudo pip` is really discouraged in the python world, yet we don't have any other way to install this globally. Using virtualenvs to create isolated environments is OK for python devs but is not a viable option for all end users. So I'd like to move on **creating a homebrew formula** sooner than later to at least get OS X users into the fray without much hassle.

Some decision points

1. Should we just [maintain our own tap](https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew/blob/master/share/doc/homebrew/How-to-Create-and-Maintain-a-Tap.md) (really just a repo with a Ruby formula in it) or try to work with the Homebrew team to get mapbox [included in the homebrew repo](https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew/blob/master/share/doc/homebrew/How-To-Open-a-Homebrew-Pull-Request-(and-get-it-merged).md)? The advantage to a Tap  is greater independence and lightweight  publishing and maintenance (just git push). The advantage to the main homebrew repo is easier discoverability, tighter quality control and slightly less typing to install (`brew install mapbox` versus `brew install mapbox/repo/mapbox`). For now, *I am leaning heavily towards using the Tap method* until we work out the kinks.

2.  If we go the Tap route, what should our repo name be? We already have a [mapbox/homebrew-custom](https://github.com/mapbox/homebrew-custom) repo (making the installation command `brew install mapbox/custom/mapbox`) but we might consider starting another `homebrew-____` repo for this and other "official" Mapbox apps.

Besides that, I've read through the [Python for Formula Authors](https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew/blob/master/share/doc/homebrew/Python-for-Formula-Authors.md) docs and have a solid working formula ready to go once we decide on these details. 

/cc @sgillies @tmcw

> So there is one more problem in the tests with throwing the right exception. I want to fix it this week. But the automatic conversion in the builder now works and is tested

After some thinking, I realize what the problem is. `scipy_fftpack` does not support `long double` and raises an exception. `numpy.fft` seems to support long double and doesn't raise an exception. In the tests, we use the same precisions as for numpy (see `test_pyfftw_scipy_interface.py`)

```python
if scipy.__version__ < '0.19':
    # Older scipy will raise an error for inputs of type float16, so we
    # cannot validate transforms with float16 input vs. scipy.fftpack
    complex_dtypes = pyfftw._supported_nptypes_complex
    real_dtypes = pyfftw._supported_nptypes_real
else:
    # reuse all dtypes from numpy tests (including float16)
    complex_dtypes = test_pyfftw_numpy_interface.complex_dtypes
    real_dtypes = test_pyfftw_numpy_interface.real_dtypes
```

The actual failure is that the original scipy and our plugin interface should raise the same exception but our interface doesn't raise one. I don't understand how this passed before.

I see two options:

1. we don't test long double on the scipy interface and accept that our interface behaves slightly differently. 
2. I add code that captures every call with `long double` in the interface. This will become a bit ugly because right now all calls end up in `_Xfftn` where the source interface of the call is not known currently.

I prefer option 1. Other precisions are not treated exactly the same, either. This is mentioned in the doc string in `numpy_fft.py`

```
It is notable that unlike :mod:`numpy.fftpack`, these functions will generally
return an output array with the same precision as the input array, and the
transform that is chosen is chosen based on the precision of the input array.
That is, if the input array is 32-bit floating point, then the transform will
be 32-bit floating point and so will the returned array. Half precision input
will be converted to single precision. Otherwise, if any type conversion is
required, the default will be double precision. If pyFFTW was not built with
support for double precision, the default is long double precision. If that is not
available, it defaults to single precision.
```

For secrets - the Rackspace API key is encrypted to your repository key (I have to do that, because I have the Rackspace API key). Hi Wenzel,

You can see the code [here](https://github.com/jmabille/pybind11/blob/numpy_array/include/pybind11/numpy_array.h) (Sorry for the formatting, I have to fix that).

The code is not totally semantically correct (especially for the resize functions) but it gives an idea on how things could work. I've added a quick implementation of wrappers on 2-dimensional arrays that satisfies my need, however a more generic implementation should be provided (as soon as I have the time to do it), providing a interface similar to boost.multiarray. Strided iterators (similar to the ones I implemented for pybind11 vectorize functionality) should also be provided.

The  need of the shared_ptr change may not be obvious in the current implementation since I only store the buffer and its size, however for N-dimensional arrays, we can't store a copy of a buffer_info object, that would  lead to a double call to PyBuffer_Release in the buffer_info destructor. Without any change in buffer_info, we'd have to store a copy of every member of buffer_info in the wrapper (except the view), and I guess you would agree it is not the right thing to do.

So somehow the buffer_info copies need to remember they share a common view and the shared_ptr seems a natural solution. Now I agree it may not be the best solution, it is just the most obvious one to avoid refactoring the buffer_info and array classes.

Johan
