I think allowing {hi} in the middle of a session is the simplest solution: https://github.com/tinode/chat/pull/51

> let say now, I send message using {pub}, then the ctrl {ctrl} comes before {data}. App will refresh the table first because {ctrl} comes first, and new message is not shown, because it comes late

That's not how it's supposed to work. A user enters a message and presses [send]. The message is first queued for delivery: it may take a second or two to deliver it to the server. You may want to show the message in the "pending" state while it's waiting to be sent, like displaying an hourglass icon. Then the {ctrl} arrives to indicate that the server has accepted the message. You can indicate it by showing single checkmark. Then *optionally* the {data} arrives. It may not arrive at all if {pub noecho=true} or if the topic does not have an 'R' permission.

You should schedule a refresh whenever you receive a {data} message because they may come not just from you but from the other users too. It does not matter if {data} is sent by you or someone else, you should react to it equally. That's why {data} has no `id`.

To get the resource pool of the destination host and get relocate working I made the following changes:

managed_entity = get_obj(content, [vim.ManagedEntity], inputs['destination_host'])
resource_pool = managed_entity.resourcePool
relocate_spec = vim.vm.RelocateSpec(pool=resource_pool, host=destination_host, datastore=destination_datastore)
task = vm.Relocate(spec=relocate_spec)

I just tested this on a single host directly (no vCenter), and there is no explicit resource pool, still the API complains about the same thing. I don't think it's a big deal, though, since I can always assign it to the default "Resources" pool.

Cool! Sorry for the late reply, this looks good at first glance! We should just take care to mimic the scipy conventions, if possible. So it should be `nearest`, not `clip`, and there's a fine distinction between `mirror` and `reflect` (https://mail.scipy.org/pipermail/scipy-user/2008-November/018611.html) we should get right (although we don't need both).

This happens on `import theano` (because Theano itself auto-imports the old module). We should update the imports for the pooling layers and update the Theano version in `requirements.txt` and `__init__.py` accordingly, though. When we did that, we could install a `warnings.filterwarning` in `__init__.py` to silence the warning, but I think we shouldn't (users might combine Lasagne with vanilla Theano and should still be warned about this).

> I changed `stride` to `st` to be more consistent with `ds`, and because it can work for both 1D and 2D, but   if you prefer `stride` and `strides` I can change it to that.

Unfortunately this makes it inconsistent with the `Conv*DLayer`s, because they use `stride` / `strides`. I think we should at least try to be consistent within the library, so if we change it here, we will have to change it there as well.

re: decoder. 
My intuition on creating a decoder was the have the hid_init be the output of the encoder and sequences always start with a start symbol.  The training would be the same as the other network I've been referencing would be (where each time step's output is treated independently).

`hid_init` and `cell_init` can be Theano expressions, so you can set them to anything you want, including the output of an MLP. Note that the output shape of that MLP must be `(batch_size, num_rnn_units)`. You will need to build the MLP's output expressions before constructing the RNN:

```python
from lasagne.layers import InputLayer, DenseLayer, LSTMLayer
mlp = InputLayer(...)
mlp = DenseLayer(mlp, num_units=100)
mlp = DenseLayer(mlp, num_units=N)
mlp_out = lasagne.layers.get_output(mlp)
rnn = InputLayer(...)
rnn = LSTMLayer(rnn, num_units=N, hid_init=mlp_out, ...)
```

And for training, you will need to obtain the parameters of both networks:
```python
params = lasagne.layers.get_all_params(mlp, trainable=True) + lasagne.layers.get_all_params(rnn, trainable=True)
updates = lasagne.updates.nesterov_momentum(loss, params, ...)
```

So this is very likely down to a bug that I don't yet know how to solve.

Basically, the autodetection of whether the input is JSON or YAML is done by first seeing if it can parse the input as JSON. If that fails, it tries parsing as YAML.

The JSON parser is very strict so an extra comma can throw it out and it gets parsed as YAML. JSON (even JSON with extraneous commas) is valid YAML so cfn-flip is doing the "correct" thing in converting what it sees as YAML into JSON.

I'd recommend running your input through a JSON linter first such as the one at https://jsonlint.com/

1. The template can become quite large if there's more than a few stepfunctions defined. 
2. It could work, but we are working in an automated integration & delivery manner. 
3. It sounds similar to what we were trying to achieve, with Transform/Include and it proofs to have some limitations. 

Since I have Enterprise Support plan as well, I asked for support through that channel also. 
We got advice through that channel, that it is possible to use Transform/Include with StepFunction definitions, in CloudFormation. 

The trick is to escape the StepFunction DefinitionString property, and include the actual property, DefinitionString, in the external CloudFormation referenced file. Escaping only the stepfunction definition string would fail, CloudFormation complaining that the referenced Transform/Include template, is not a valid yaml/json. 
Here's how it looks like:
Template:
```
StepFunction1: 
    Type: "AWS::StepFunctions::StateMachine"
    Properties:
      StateMachineName: !Ref StepFunction1SampleName
      RoleArn: !GetAtt StepFunctionExecutionRole.Arn      
      Fn::Transform:
        Name: AWS::Include
        Parameters:
          Location: 
            Fn::Sub: 's3://${ArtifactsBucketName}/StepFunctions/StepFunction1/definition.json'
```
External stepfunction definition file:
```
{
    "DefinitionString" : {"Fn::Sub" : "{\r\n  \"Comment\": \"A Retry example of the Amazon States Language using an AWS Lambda Function\",\r\n  \"StartAt\": \"HelloWorld\",\r\n  \"States\": {\r\n    \"HelloWorld\": {\r\n      \"Type\": \"Task\",\r\n      \"Resource\": \"arn:aws:lambda:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:function:${HelloWorldLambdaFunctionName}\",      \r\n      \"End\": true\r\n    }\r\n  }\r\n}"}
}
```

Now, although this solves the problem, it's a bit more difficult to maintain the StepFunction definition, in this form, in source control. 

So I've thought about using a CloudFormation custom resource backed by a lambda function. The lambda function would deal with the actual StepFunction DefinitionString escaping part. 

Here's how it looks like:
Template:
```
StepFunctionParser:
    Type: Custom::AMIInfo
    Properties:
      ServiceToken: myLambdaArn
      DefinitionString: 
        Fn::Transform:
          Name: AWS::Include
          Parameters:
            Location: 
              Fn::Sub: 's3://${ArtifactsBucketName}/StepFunctions/StepFunctionX/definition.json'   
  StepFunctionX: 
    Type: "AWS::StepFunctions::StateMachine"
    Properties:
      StateMachineName: StepFunction1SampleNameX
      RoleArn: !GetAtt StepFunctionExecutionRole.Arn      
      DefinitionString: !GetAtt StepFunctionParser.DefinitionString
```
External StepFunction definition file:
```
{
  "Comment": "A Retry example of the Amazon States Language using an AWS Lambda Function",
  "StartAt": "HelloWorld",
  "States": {
    "HelloWorld": {
      "Type": "Task",
      "Resource": {"Fn::Sub" : "arn:aws:lambda:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:function:${HelloWorldLambdaFunctionName}" },
      "End": true
    }
  }
}
```
Here's a [https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/walkthrough-custom-resources-lambda-lookup-amiids.html](documentation link) for creating AWS Lambda-backed Custom Resources. 

There's still a problem with this.
Transform/Include converts external template boolean properties into string properties. 
Therefore, DefinitionString
```
"DefinitionString": {
            "States": {
                "HelloWorld": {
                    "Type": "Task",
                    "Resource": "arn:aws:lambda:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:function:${HelloWorldLambdaFunctionName}",
                    **"End": true**
                }
            },
            "Comment": "A Retry example of the Amazon States Language using an AWS Lambda Function",
            "StartAt": "HelloWorld"
        }
```
becomes
```
"DefinitionString": {
            "States": {
                "HelloWorld": {
                    "Type": "Task",
                    "Resource": _realLambdaFunctionArn_,
                    **"End": "true"**
                }
            },
            "Comment": "A Retry example of the Amazon States Language using an AWS Lambda Function",
            "StartAt": "HelloWorld"
        }
```

CloudFormation then complains about the StepFunction definition not being valid:
```
Invalid State Machine Definition: 'SCHEMA_VALIDATION_FAILED: Expected value of type Boolean at /States/HelloWorld/End' 
```
Is this a CloudFormation Transform/Include issue? Can someone from AWS give a statement on this?

Thanks for the logs. Certbot's behavior is more or less identical in each file until you pass/fail the challenge. I am not certain I know what the problem is, but I have a strong hunch.

I suspect this problem is due to [authorization reuse](https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/upcoming-change-valid-authz-reuse/16982) by Let's Encrypt. Basically, before Certbot can get a certificate from Let's Encrypt, it needs to prove that you control the domain you requested. Once you have done this, Let's Encrypt remembers that you are authorized for that domain for a period of time based on their current policies. The result of this is if you ask for a certificate again while you still have a valid authorization, the authorization is reused and they give you a certificate without actually checking that you performed the challenge properly.

When running Certbot with `--dry-run`, Certbot talks to [Let's Encrypt staging server](https://letsencrypt.org/docs/staging-environment/) which does not share authorizations with the production server. I suspect Certbot is failing to complete the challenge, but you have a valid authorization on the production server so Let's Encrypt isn't actually checking that you've solved the challenge correctly.

If this is the case, you should take some time to investigate these errors as `certbot renew --force-renewal` will fail in the future when your authorization expires. To start with, I would check the things suggested in Certbot's output in your `--dry-run` log:

"To fix these errors, please make sure that your domain name was entered correctly and the DNS A record(s) for that domain contain(s) the right IP address. Additionally, please check that your computer has a publicly routable IP address and that no firewalls are preventing the server from communicating with the client. If you're using the webroot plugin, you should also verify that you are serving files from the webroot path you provided."
@ohemorange, can you add this to your review queue?

Both errors are actually due to the problem `from urllib2 import build_opener, HTTPHandler, HTTPSHandler`. The other error is a [fall back](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/letsencrypt-auto-source/pieces/pipstrap.py#L49) for the same problem and is trying to use code that works in Python 3, but `certbot-auto` doesn't use Python 3 (unless you're setting the environment variable `LE_PYTHON` and doing other work yourself).

`urllib2.HTTPSHandler` is part of the [Python standard library](https://docs.python.org/2/library/urllib2.html#urllib2.HTTPSHandler). It's possible these files may be corrupted on your system. Have you installed any software on this server using `pip`?

To see if you can reproduce the problem outside of Certbot, can you run:
```
VENV_DIR=$(mktemp -d)
virtualenv --no-site-packages -p $(command -v python2.7 || command -v python27 || command -v python2 || command -v python) "$VENV_DIR"
"$VENV_DIR/bin/python" -c 'from urllib2 import build_opener, HTTPHandler, HTTPSHandler'
rm -rf "$VENV_DIR"
```

The reason the `renew` subcommand is strictly non-interactive is largely historical. We also expect/want people to automate certificate renewal wherever possible though. Due to the otherwise largely automated nature of Certbot and Let's Encrypt and the short 3 month certificate lifetimes, making renewal automated makes using Certbot much less error prone and easy to use.

With that said, we can potentially look into allowing interactivity with `renew` (through something like the `--force-interactive` flag as Noah suggested), but I have a few options for you that might resolve this for you:

1. Ideally, you can use [manual hooks](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#hooks) to automate renewal for you. This allows you to execute arbitrary commands to set up and clean up the challenge. Certbot sets environment variables with all the information needed to perform the challenge.
2. If the above option doesn't work, you can use `certbot certonly` (or `certbot run` if you're also using an [installer](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#getting-certificates-and-choosing-plugins) like our Apache or Nginx plugins). How exactly you should use this depends on your configuration and workflow. You can use the `--cert-name` flag to specify which certificate to renew. The name you'd use here is the name of the directory in `/etc/letsencrypt/live` containing the certificate. Any questions that Certbot asks you can be answered on the command line. For example, if your certificate is not close to expiration, Certbot will ask you if you want to exit or renew the cert anyway. You can choose between these options with the `--keep` or `--force-renewal` flags respectively.

@SwartzCr, I don't think this requires a bug fix release as this has always been the behavior of `certbot renew`. We can consider allowing interactivity in the future though if my suggestions above don't solve the problem.

> We may also want to rework the docs section on this to stop recommending -n since it's no longer necessary: https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#renewing-certificates

I don't think we recommend `-n` for `renew`. We do if you're renewing your cert with `certonly` though.

@pconrad-fb, thanks for your questions; I've tried to answer them below.

> Are letsencrypt and certbot equivalent (except that cerbot is the preferred term now)?

The project used to be called letsencrypt but was renamed to certbot. It is the same codebase, but some people will have old packages and/or scripts and/or third-party guides that refer to the old name.

> From denaje, is this correct:
> If your Linux distro's package manager supports letsencrypt, install it from there and use the letsencrypt binary. ( should be certbot binary? )

Should probably be certbot everywhere, although a small number of OS package managers will also still use the old name.

> If not, then clone the git repo and use the certbot-auto binary.

Right.

> Seems like we should make it clear what the plugins do--maybe even on the front page where the dropdowns are. Otherwise, people who are using "none of the above" get dumped into a place where they have to figure out what "certonly" means. I'm ruminating on a way to do this without breaking that nice clean interface. Perhaps a link at the bottom that says "My operating system or webserver is not listed" and links to an explanation of certbot-auto, description of the process at a high level, and a list of plugins?

Yeah, something like that might be useful.

> From denaje: "What does 'obtain a cert' mean? I think it means that it creates as certificate on the letsencrypt servers after proving domain ownership, downloads it, and saves it to the filesystem ; if so, this could be made clearer. So, these plugins can be used with the 'certonly' command...can they also be used without the 'certonly' command? Why would I use 'certonly' versus leaving it out? There is still no clear explanation of what this does." So...this could go on the new page linked from the front page as described in 3. But I don't know the answers to his questions.

Obtaining a cert means saving it in the filesystem, while installing it means modifying the configuration file of some server application (such as Apache or Nginx, or others) to refer to that certificate and cause it to be used for inbound TLS connections.

Plugins can be used with `certonly` or with other commands. Some commands don't necessarily need a plugin (an example is `register`). A command *must* be used whenever `certbot` is run (the default command, if none is specified, is `run`, which attempts both obtaining and installing a cert).

The point of `certonly` is to obtain a cert without installing it. This is either because ① you don't need or want an installer to be used now, or you're not ready to install the cert, or you don't trust the installer associated with the server you use, or ② you want to renew or replace an individual existing cert that is already installed, or ③ no useful installer is available for you. I can see that one of these things (renewing individual certs) is not exactly like the others, although from our point of view as Certbot developers, they are all analogous because they only authenticate and cause cert issuance in a lineage, without then invoking installation code.

> We also need to define what the cert installation process does versus what "authenticators" do. Can you provide more detail?

The authenticators are plugins that are used to prove to the CA that the user is entitled to obtain certs for a particular domain name or domain names because the user controls the name(s) in question. In order to provide this proof, they need to perform some kind of configuration change that is specified by the CA and also visible to the CA (which is called "completing a challenge"). Authenticators know how to perform one or more challenges; the way that they do this varies from authenticator to authenticator and could include things like changing a file on the system, changing a web server configuration file, spawning a temporary web server, giving instructions to a human user about a configuration change that should be made by hand, and (in the future) perhaps uploading a file to a different server or changing entries in a DNS zone. The appropriate authenticator to use depends on how you want to prove control over names, and which ones are practical options depends on the particular system and context (for example, which web server, if any, is already running).

> It's not immediately clear what is the difference between certbot and certbot-auto, and I can't find a man page for certbot-auto.

It depends on how you installed it. If you have an operating system package, you have `certbot`. If you installed it from git, you have `certbot-auto`. They take the same options and users are always supposed to run the commands using the forms that they have. (Another distinction is that `certbot-auto` is usually not in your `PATH` but `certbot` usually is.)

> Need to know more about the --standalone and --webroot options for both getting and renewing certs.

Those are both plugins. The standalone plugin creates its own temporary webserver in order to perform the authentication step (responding to challenges); the webroot plugin instead responds to challenges by writing files into a specified directory (the "webroot") for each domain, so that an existing webserver that's already running on the system will be able to serve those files at the called-for locations when the CA asks for them.

If you're not running a webserver on your system at all, standalone is great because it will make one for you (and shut it down when it's done). If you are already running a webserver, webroot is probably the best choice as long as the webserver is somehow serving files out of the filesystem (as opposed to out of a database or something). An advantage of webroot is that it works without any specific knowledge of webserver integration, so it will work well today with non-Apache webservers as well as Apache (though in that case only with certonly because webroot does not offer an installer!).

> Need to really understand certonly.

certonly is named in contrast to the idea of obtaining *and* installing a cert; it is "cert only" because it *only* obtains the cert. As I noted above, you can also use it for individual-cert renewals, whether due to expiry or due to a change in the content of the cert, or if you want to change the defaults for how the cert will be renewed in the future (e.g., to change which plugin will be used for renewal). certonly always saves the most recent settings that were used to obtain an individual cert in the renewal configuration file for that lineage.

> Can you verify that these statements from denaje are true: If you don't specify a command, then the command is "run".

Yes.

> "install a cert in your current webserver" implies that it's going to mess with my server's config files, thus defining what "install" means.

Yes.

> certonly obtains, but does not install. This means that it will authenticate with the letsencrypt server, generate the certificate, and download it to my filesystem, but it will not mess with my webserver's config files.

Yes.

> What is the behavior then if I specify certonly with --apache or --nginx? Does that combination of parameters even make sense?

Yes, those plugins provide an authenticator which attempts to satisfy challenges by changing web server configurations (what's now called the TLS-SNI-01 challenge method). In that case, the plugin will be used to perform these changes in order to respond to challenges from the CA. This is an alternative to using the webroot plugin in this context. You would need to have the corresponding web server installed and running.

> "run" is the same as "certonly" followed by "install".

Yes. (I think there might be some minor details about this, but this is generally the way we think of it.)

Let me elaborate a bit here:

Certbot uses a third party library for configuration management, Augeas. Augeas is used to build DOM tree that is convinient to search, pull and push values from/to. Augeas also parses the comment lines to be able to add / remove comments from the configuration. In Certbot, the configuration file is passed to Augeas, which then should return the traversable DOM element. Unfortunately in this case, Augeas fails parsing some of the lines in your configuration, and reports the line it failed on back to Certbot.

What I'm trying to do here, is to understand what character or sequence of characters caused the parsing error in Augeas in order to report it upstream or try to mitigate it somehow in Augeas parameters or configuration on Certbot end. Just to answer a few of your questions quickly:
* the placeholders are saved through the standard Django formset/inline admin mechanism. That is `PlaceholderEditorInline` and `PlaceholderInlineFormSet`.
* FYI, in `BaseContentItemFormSet`, the `save_new()` and `save_existing()` are overwritten to deal with placeholders too. Those calls are initiated by the standard `BaseModelFormSet.save()`.
* When you change the layout in the client-side (e.g. using the layout box), the client-side will detect which placeholders will become orphaned, and mark those for deletion. It adds a `placeholder-fs-#-DELETE` field so the object is removed on save.
* The Django formset code in `BaseModelFormSet.save_existing_objects()` loops through `BaseFormSet.deleted_forms`. You may want to use that to your advantage to remove orphaned objects.

When writing this code, I've uses step-through debugging in PyCharm to get this code working, because the inner workings of the formset/inlineadmin code is complex. By walking through the formset code - even through `django.forms.models` - this allowed implementing all the save logic without having to resort to completely rewriting `ModelAdmin.change_view()` like others do, since that is horrible to begin with.

It was mentioned that `**change` might be a problem if new content were ever added to the change dict. However I think that can be mitigated by tacking `**kwargs` on the end of change handler definitions to catch unused arguments. That way you can specify as many or as few arguments as might be needed without creating problems for future updates:

```python
def change_handler(name, old, new, owner, **kwargs):
    # do something with name, old, new, and owner

```python
def change_handler(new, **kwargs):
    # do something just with new
```

When you save a notebook you have run on one computer, it saves a signature in the database to record that the contents of the current notebook are trusted. If you then change it on another computer, it has a different signature, which is not in the database, and so it shows up as untrusted. With this scheme, sharing the secret key is not enough to share 'trust' between the two computers.

When we first added a trust mechanism, we stored the signature in the notebook file itself; with this, sharing the static secret key file was enough to share trust. But it was annoying for version control, and in the default case (without doing anything to share trust), merely opening a notebook and saving it again with no changes rewrites the signature, so it's even easier to make the notebook 'untrusted' on a system which should trust it. For this reason, we changed to the sqlite signature store.

It's possible to plug in your own signature store, if you're willing to run a database server that all of your computers can access. Unfortunately, that's the only really robust way we've so far come up with to share trust between computers, and it's obviously not practical for many users.

@JoelBondurant If you are presenting code and don't need to actually execute it (which is presumably what you would be doing if you're hiding the code) you can use `nbconvert`.  

How exactly you would use it would depend on what exactly you want. 

For example, if you wanted to explore a notebook as though you were going through the notebook but without having to see or execute any of the code, you could export to html and use something like @parente's solution to hide all of the input cells (as a PR https://github.com/jupyter/nbviewer/pull/681 and as a [blogpost](http://mindtrove.info/code-hiding-on-nbviewer/). 

If you want a bit more flexibility (e.g., showing some inputs, but not all; or being able to toggle the visibility of individual cells' input), you could use the approach I took in https://github.com/mpacer/hiding_tags_nbconvert. In that case, you would tag all the cells that for which the input would be eliminated with `kill_input` (and you would use the `kill_hidden.tpl` template). That would solve your problem in the same way as above, but on a per cell basis. 

However, suppose someone asked a question specifically about the code, you might want to make it accessible; that wouldn't be possible if the cells were actually not present(which is what the `kill_hidden.tpl` does… thus the ominous moniker of `kill`). However, you might want to only apply this to some cells, and you might not want to have to toggle all the cells at once in order to show just one (which may cause scrolling issues for that one cell). Then you want the `toggle_hidden.tpl` template, which creates per-cell visibility that is controlled by a toggle control (e.g., see the checkbox next to `{%- block any_cell -%}` at [this example site](https://mpacer.github.io/hiding_tags_nbconvert/hide_cells_based_on_tags.html) for to see what I mean). In that case, you would tag code cells with `hide_input` and would use the `toggle_hidden.tpl`. 

If you wanted to have a blending of the two, that's just a matter of me writing a new template which wouldn't be hard, but won't a priority for me unless someone specifically asks for it.

Python comes standard on most *nix distributions, golang does not. `get get` and `pip install` are comparable from a package manager perspective (although I wouldn't classify `brew install` as the same) as they are both package managers used with specific languages. From my perspective one of the main benefits of using golang over python was the ability to compile static binaries that do not require external dependencies. In our case we wish to run SOPS on servers and not just local dev workstations. Installing golang, pulling sops source code from github, pulling other external dependencies in a source code, compiling the code, and finally running the binary on a server (especially for a language that can compile to binaries) is not what I would call good practice. Also what happens in situtions where a server only whitelists certain domains, asking operations teams to whitelist github.com in order for software to work on a server is going to be an uphill fight (and one that will end up being lost by devs).

`go get` would make perfect sense if SOPS was purely a library and not a CLI tool. `pip install` is required for python packages and makes sense because the user MUST have python to run the tool anyways as python is an interpreted language. Ruby has the same problem. There are tools written such as `virtualenv` just to help get around some of the odd issues that come up with this configuration. Some software written with these languages even ship an embedded version of python or ruby just to get around the headache.

Sure! Actually, one of the things I did for Bleach 2.0 was restructure Bleach's sanitizer so it was easier to compare the two. Sounds like I didn't do a good enough job on that. Sorry!

Bleach's sanitizer allows for a map for `ALLOWED_ATTRIBUTES` giving you a lot more control over sanitizing attributes: you can sanitize attributes for specific tags, you can sanitize based on value, etc.

Bleach's sanitizer always alphabetizes attributes, but uses an alphabetizer that works with namespaces--the html5lib one is broken in that regard.

Bleach's sanitizer always quotes attribute values because that's the safe thing to do. The html5lib one makes that configurable. In this case, Bleach doesn't make something configurable that isn't safe.

Bleach's sanitizer has a very restricted set of `ALLOWED_PROTOCOLS` by default. html5lib has a much more expansive one that I claim is less safe.

`html5lib.filters.sanitizer.Filter`'s `sanitize_css` is broken and doesn't work.

There are probably some other differences, but they're more minor.

Hope that helps!

@scottpurdy Sorry, I'll break down the main issue at hand and some suggestions. 

Certain Python packages on Windows require [Python Tools for Visual Studio](https://www.visualstudio.com/en-us/features/python-vs.aspx) (PTVS) to build them from source. NumPy is a pain with it's requirement for a certain linear algebra library/package (BLAS or LINPACK). And we know the pycapnp package is not currently available for Windows. Plus there are other Windows specific packages such as PyWin32. 
So, source building for some of the binding and nupic requirements can be done, but others don't exist or are specific to Windows. The following are potential work-arounds that I'm unsure of and require help -
- Enforce Pip is the most recent version when installing bindings and nupic (so that [requirement specifiers](http://pip.readthedocs.org/en/stable/reference/pip_install/#requirement-specifiers) can be used).
- Work with Jason Paryani and co. on adding Windows support to pycapnp.
- Separate out the two requirements.txt to have Linux and Windows version, modifying bindings and nupic setup.py to pick the right one.

Now that pycapnp is a requirement of the nupic.bindings and nupic, the second option could be done. With the third option quicker, but seems a temporary work-around.

@scottpurdy, regarding
> In general, I am a little wary of the common flags that are applied to externals as well. Compiler settings sometimes vary by project so we may need to use a different set for some of the externals (I think).

This was a solution to a real problem. There are a number of settings that we need to have in common, and as I was introducing new external lib builds into nupic.core build, I was getting linker errors. For example, `-fPIC` is needed for producing position-independent code for DLLs, and all objects that will be linked into a DLL need to be compiled with that flag. Then, there are BITNESS flags, optimization flags, debugging-symbol generation flags, etc.  It was impossible to maintain consistency between modules without a way to share settings across modules, and do this correctly everywhere across compiler toolchains and OS platforms.

> I hold my previous comment about referencing task by python package.module.class name not Luigi's one.

I agree that it makes sense in many settings. But I'm not sure we should remove the support for it, hence forcing that new convention. I'm afraid it have implications we haven't fully thought about. We could however:

 * Make it as a configurable default to set the `task_namespace` to be the same as package+module. Big organizations can then turn this on by default in config then.
 * Extend `TaskParameter` to silently "try" do the module loading. If we also change the current command line parsing (which I want to do anyway), making the `--task` param a `TaskParameter`, we would allow users to shorten cron lines from `--module my_module --task my_module.MyTask`  to only `--task my_module.MyTask`.

What do you think about that? I think we could keep the `task_namespace` concept in luigi though for those who want it.

print("Y shape:", Y.shape)

model.fit(X, Y, validation_set=.1, show_metric=True, batch_size=32)

    from klein import Klein

   ...

    from app.routes.api import *

With a very fresh build of Z3 (i.e., out of github master branch HEAD), I see the reported behavior:

```
$ z3 -version
Z3 version 4.8.0 - 64 bit
```

Perhaps you're using an older version?

	else if (S == 2.0) Output = 2.0;
	else if (S == 3.0) Output = 3.0;
	else if (S == 4.0) Output = 4.0;
	else if (S == 5.0) Output = 5.0;
	else if (S == 6.0) Output = 6.0;

}

I have ruminated on solutions for more elegant modification of the `Chain` object and all of it's various dependencies.  Ideally, it'd be nice to be able to swap out the deeply buried `Transaction` class for a specific VM (`Chain -> VM -> VMState -> Block -> Transaction`) with a simple one-liner API.

```python
MainnetChain.new_magic_configuration_method(
    'frontier.vm.vm_state.block.transaction_class',
    MySpecialTransactionClass,
)
```

This would allow this sort of *deep* modification of the configuration such as injecting a *mixin* class with an overridden `apply_computation` method into *all* of the `Computation` classes.  At that point, it'd be easy for the `Computation` class to expose the functionality you suggest in the `post_opcode_callback`.

@ldng Sure you should be able to use constance without the admin. Simply use the `constance.config` object to set the value. Don't write to the database directly via the model since there is caching involved and setting values directly will prevent the cache invalidation to work correctly. This is by design since the backends are implementation details and differ in the way they work behind the scenes (Django ORM vs. Redis commands).

So this should work for you:

```python
from constance import config
config.WEBSERVICE = 'http://...'
```

another way of handling with (while avoiding the duplicate entries on `sys.path`) would be to simply add `sys.path.insert(1, sys.path.pop())` after the `site.addsitedir` call.

However, not modifying `sys.path` in this way was a deliberate choice on our part...

At LinkedIn we distribute a "clean" python (e.g. no third party libraries installed), and we have automation to ensure that no packages are installed into it (such that it's site-packages remains untainted), which gives us the luxury of being able to rely solely on the standard library's `site` module for setting up shiv's environment. 

I definitely realize that this sort of infrastructure is not always available, let me think on this a bit and do some research. Thanks in advance for your patience!

So the facts seems to be:

* `authGSSClientInit()` in WinKerberos ends up ignoring the `principal` argument.
* `authGSSClientInit()` in WinKerberos accepts `user`, `domain`, and `password`, which aren't recognized by PyKerberos.
* Support for `principal` can be emulated when using WinKerberos by splitting on '@' to get the user and domain arguments. But should requests-kerberos do this, or WinKerberos?
* The password *may* be supplied when using the `principal` argument with WinKerberos.

I feel like the change on the requests-kerberos side is shaping up as recognizing a `principal` value in the form of `user[:password]@domain` on Windows, or alternatively adding a `password` argument that will only be recognized on Windows. Does this sound reasonable?

> Without sshuttle running:
>
>     curl: (7) Failed to connect to X port 8080: Connection refused
>
> With:
>
>     curl: (52) Empty reply from server

This is completely normal and 100% expected.

As sshuttle operates at the Application layer, not the network layer, by
the time it works out the server has rejected the connection it is too
late to send a "Connection refused" type message as we have already
established a TCP connection. So we have no choice but to close the
connection without transferring any data.

Ok. I see what's up. I bet you ran this on Python version 3.5.x (or more precisely < Python 3.6) where `open()` doesn't accept a Path type.

One simple fix is to change: 

```
exec(open(version_path, 'r').read())
```
to: 
```
exec(open(str(version_path), 'r').read())
```

but perhaps this error has some blessings in disguise. (Or at least I hope you take it that way :-) )

Previously when I was checking to see if this worked for Pypy, I recall reading that Mythril needs 3.6 or later. Is 3.5 acceptable? 

Our setup.py suggest yes [here](https://github.com/ConsenSys/mythril/blob/master/setup.py#L51).

However it also says [here](https://github.com/ConsenSys/mythril/blob/master/setup.py#L297-L302) that Python 2x, 2.7, 3x, .3, and 3.4 are allowed in addition. And no mention of 3.6 or 3.7. 

So which versions do we support? 

After understanding that, I would be happy to correct all of the parts of setup.py to make them consistent.

                doc = [token for token in doc if not ignore(token)]
            seq = [0] * pad
            seq += [(token.lex_id or token.orth) for token in doc]
            seq += [0] * pad
            seqs.append(ops.asarray(seq, dtype='uint64'))
        return seqs, None
    return layerize(forward)
```

ah, what's the motivation here?

I've updated the cursor scheme to use shorter cursors – instead of dumping everything to JSON, then base64-encoding, I base64-encode each separate value as a string, remove the padding, then join with the unused `.` character.

In general, this leads to shorter cursors, as I can avoid encoding quotes for strings, and need only a single byte to encode dividers.

This format also opens up the possibility of directly encoding binary values to base64 instead of strings down the road.

Here's a contrived example of the performance increase when using the python callback. It's pretty extreme in this scenario, but I'm hoping someone with lots of real rules on lots of real files can use this to get a more accurate measurement. Things like metadata and strings will add more time to the callback setup because it has to convert those into python objects too.

Here's a simple script which shows it in use:
```
wxs@wxs-mbp yara-python % cat test.py
import os
import yara

def callback(data):
    global count
    count += 1
    return yara.CALLBACK_CONTINUE

data = 'A' * 200

rule = ''
for i in range(2000):
    rule += 'rule rule_%s { condition: false }' % i

rules = yara.compile(source=rule)
scanned = 0
count = 0
matches = 0
for i in range(2000):
    scanned += 1
    matches += len(rules.match(data=data, callback=callback, which=yara.CALLBACK_NON_MATCHES))
print('Scanned: %s' % scanned)
print('Counter: %s' % count)
print('Matches: %s' % matches)
```

This scans the same 200 bytes, 2000 times, each time with 2000 rules. In this case it will call the callback for non-matches (so 2000 * 2000 times). On my laptop this takes anywhere from 4.7 to 5.7 seconds. This particular run is on the higher end:

```
wxs@wxs-mbp yara-python % PYTHONPATH=build/lib.macosx-10.12-intel-2.7 /usr/bin/time python -S ./test.py
Scanned: 2000
Counter: 4000000
Matches: 0
        5.74 real         5.43 user         0.16 sys
wxs@mbp yara-python %
```

If I change the `which` argument to be `yara.CALLBACK_MATCHES` we call the callback 0 times and things go much faster:

```
wxs@wxs-mbp yara-python % PYTHONPATH=build/lib.macosx-10.12-intel-2.7 /usr/bin/time python -S ./test.py
Scanned: 2000
Counter: 0
Matches: 0
        0.16 real         0.14 user         0.01 sys
wxs@wxs-mbp yara-python %
```

As I said above, this is a highly contrived example, but I think it illustrates the point.

An externally created proxy will only be picked up by Ganga if it matches the requirements. If it really is dodgy in some unspecified way then it can destroyed as mentioned above.

It's worth noting that `voms-proxy-destroy` is really just a call to `rm`, it's nothing special.

Firstly:
There is _nothing_ stopping a simpler and more generic method being written than the `execCmd` within the `GaudiExec` app.
This `execCmd` method is _always_ intended to be run against the app on disk the user has configured as they may have local modifications and we can _never_ know they don't have local changes against any LHCb code. If the user chooses to make no local modifications then unfortunately they still need the app in a writable directory to make the targets required by the `GaudiExec` app.
The `execCmd` is __not__ intended to be a be-all/end-all way of accessing LHCb applications/environment, else it would have been a static function and not dependent on the specific application folder the user has requested.

As for generating the XML, yes this needs to be done on the WN from within the env which has been setup by the user to correctly pull in any potentially local changes. This needs to be added as a minor extra step in the `GaudiExec` app and is potentially only a small amount of work. Merging can be done using either the same env as the users app (imo best for consistency) or any other env the user wants to use.

Thanks @bukepo, that helps my understanding greatly. I see you are trying to write blocking API calls that combine both sending the request and parsing the response. Introducing this method makes for much more elegant code, so I can see your motivation.

I'm going to give a little more thought to this and respond more decisively later today.

Regarding @abtink's comment:

> We need to ensure that for a failed case, the passed-in variables remain unchanged.

This was never a guarantee, even with the standard `spinel_datatype_vunpack()`. All arguments that were successfully parsed up to the point of the parse error will be changed. This generally isn't a problem as long as you can signal some sort of error condition that indicates that the frame was not parsed as expected. Unless I am missing something, I don't think there is a need to restore previous values.

Thanks for driving this effort to harmonize the whitelist, blacklist, and received signal strength filters!

Couple comments on the top-level API:
1. This is a large-enough (sub-)component to warrant its own prefix (e.g. `otLinkFilter*`).
2. For the default received signal strength, I believe we can simplify by passing `NULL` for the extended address.  When getting the default received signal strength, we could use the existing structure and just set the extended address to all `0xff`.

With the couple modifications above, we should arrive at something like:

```
otLinkFilterGetAddressMode()
otLinkFilterSetAddressMode()
otLinkFilterAddAddress()
otLinkFilterRemoveAddress()
otLinkFilterClearAddresses()
otLinkFilterGetNextAddress()

otLinkFilterAddRssIn()
otLinkFilterRemoveRssIn()
otLinkFilterClearRssIn()
otLinkFilterGetNextRssIn()
```

Thoughts?

Ah, I see what is going on.  I believe the current logic was written with the SED in mind.  For SEDs, the desire was to keep trying to send Child Update Request messages for as long as the SED's timeout.  We felt this was OK given that transmissions are naturally rate limited by the SED's polling.

However, the current logic fails to change that behavior for rx-on-when-idle Children.  I'm fine with adjusting the logic such that it tries only once or twice for rx-on-when-idle Children.  However, I see value in continued attempts with SEDs.

One of the challenges with IEEE 802.15.4 is that received ACKs can lead to false positives (the ACK only includes an 8-bit DSN), especially in dense environments.  That is why retransmissions are not always sufficient.  False positives occur with low probability, but we do see them in practice.

```
def pants_setup_py(name, description, additional_classifiers=None, **kwargs):
  """Creates the setup_py for a pants artifact.

  ...
  return PythonArtifact(
      name=name,
      version=pants_version(),
      description=description,
      long_description=(_read_contents('src/python/pants/ABOUT.rst') +
                        _read_contents('src/python/pants/CHANGELOG.rst')),
      url='https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants',
      license='Apache License, Version 2.0',
      zip_safe=True,
      classifiers=list(classifiers),
      **kwargs)
```

I'm thinking we could change this by:

1)  having a special function to replace `__read_contents()` to just pull the first entry of CHANGELOG.rst.

Also you need a service file (init script or systemd .service file) that starts/stops the pypiserver and loads the configuration provided by the admin. So I worked around all that by having a manually created rpm without pypiserver, but which contains a shell script (plus systemd service file). That shell script can be changed manually to provide configurable port and package location, and it starts a virtualenv and installs pypiserver into that virtualenv. The changed parameters are given to pypiserver via shell args.

>for example if pytest_deselected(items) changed to pytest_deselected(items, reason)

The question is *where* is this *changed*. If it's in the *spec* then everything will work since `warn_on_args` is also *on the spec*.

>and my conftest only calls config.ihook.pytest_deselected(items=removed)

@RonnyPfannschmidt I'm proposing this emits a warning and the call will still work - again #170.

I don't see why it's a problem that plugins **do not work** on old versions of `pytest` that don't support the `reason` arg - that's a pretty darn good reason for a new version of a plugin.

@fschulze If the *spec* doesn't define the new args then your call shouldn't either; the plugin **should** be aware of the host it is dealing with.

Trying to hack this inside the *call* machinery of `pluggy` is not correct imho. You should not be allowing a version of a plugin to work *implicitly* over multiple versions of a host without special explicit provisions (just like you would with any dependency that changes its api - pin a version of the plugin to versions of the host).

@RonnyPfannschmidt @fschulze this is the way I see it:
- you want to add a new argument
- change the `hookspec` in the host to introduce the new argument
- plugins not calling with the new argument see a future warning (under my proposal for a `warn_on_args`)
- plugins are eventually changed to include the new argument in hook calls likely via a version release or **some change**
- plugins now with the new argument can't call the old versions of the host since the old host's spec doesn't include this argument (results in an error)
- **the version pin change**: pin the new version of the plugin to only support newer versions of the host like you would with literally any other dependency API change ever..
- or do, **the hack introspection approach**: if you don't want to pin versions then simply modify the `**kwargs` to your hook call line based on introspecting the `hookspec` from the current version of the host - #57 would aid with this

Trying to make this work magically in `pluggy` I think is not correct - it should be the plugin's job (if anyone's) to figure out what API is supported in the host, not `pluggy`'s. Version pinning to the host should be a thing; it's explicit demarcation of breaking changes and  there's nothing wrong with having version(s) of a plugin tied to specific version of a host.

The difference between `hls` and `hlsvariant` is subtle, it's more for plugin authors or power users to know the difference. Personally, I'd like to remove the difference for the end-user so they don't have to know what it means. The `hlsvariant` type is where the stream describes a list of different qualities (variants) for the same stream, which is how HLS works in general. A `hls` is used for when the stream is a direct link to one of those variants.

@gravyboat I'm still interested in developing this feature. I don't have any time at the moment because of my new school. But holidays start in 2 weeks, so I might have time then. I'm thinking about making it a big change in a separate branch. Let me know what you guys think about this:

Major change for seek and multi-segment streaming support.

- Change Streamlinks behavior so it acts as a caching proxy server.
- Streamlink passes through all information it downloads from the server exactly as is, including HTTP or other protocol headers. This means that the receiving player must support the streaming protocol that is being proxied (such as RTMP/HLS/HTTP Progressive).
- This behavior would mean that specifying the transport method no longer makes sense (i.e. It doesn't make sense to output the stream to the player using a pipe or HTTP if the stream is an RTMP stream)
- Makes seek very easy as the player knows how to handle seeking for different streaming protocols. Streamlink just has to proxy requests from the player, and can make extra requests in parallel to accelerate downloads.

> could be configured to have a tag whitelist of just `` Not unreasonable, but there's still a difference: a run called "`az`" should become "`a<wbr>z`". > I was contemplating making a polymer element for a `` with wordbreaking for its content, that does the `` insertion and HTML escaping in a more reusable way Semantically, this is—in my opinion—entirely reasonable. If I were writing the code, though, I probably wouldn't write such a component: defining new components in Polymer feels extremely clunky to me, so that components are "heavyweight" instead of "lightweight," and it's not clear to me that this component pulls its weight. By that I mean: for a Polymer component, you need your own file, some DOM-module and import boilerplate, a script tag where you declare some properties, a computed property for the escaped text, and then a template with the implementation. (Contrast something like React, where the entire component is literally `export const WordBreaks = (props) => {addWordBreaks(props.children)};` in an existing `utilityComponents.ts` file or similar.) Note that I'm not asking that you _not_ implement it, just letting you know where I'm coming from. One question: does it make sense to have the same component handle ``s and HTML escaping? > have the element definition live in `tf_dashboard_common` since that seems like the right place for a generic element We're trying to get rid of `tf_dashboard_common`, because all components in `tensorboard/components/` should by definition be common across all dashboards—otherwise, they'd be in `tensorboard/plugins/*/`. Sorry to be a little pedantic, but your timeit command above will actually generate the first image even though you give it the 50 argument. Simple example:
```
In[44]: g = (i for i in range(100))
In[45]: next(islice(g, 5))
Out[45]: 0
In[46]: next(islice(g, 5))
Out[46]: 1
In[47]: next(islice(g, 5))
Out[47]: 2
In[48]: next(islice(g, 5))
Out[48]: 3
In[49]: next(islice(g, 5))
Out[49]: 4
In[50]: next(islice(g, 5))
Out[50]: 5
```
To get the 50th image in the fist shot, you need `next(islice(gen, 50, 51))`:
```
In[54]: g = (i for i in range(100))
In[55]: next(islice(g, 5, 6))
Out[55]: 5
```
But ultimately, this approach does not work for me because even though it 'skips' to the 50th image, it seems to somehow consume the earlier images, so that  they are not accessible anymore:
```
In[56]: g = (i for i in range(100))
In[57]: next(islice(g, 50, 51))
Out[57]: 50
In[58]: sum(1 for _ in g)
Out[58]: 49
```
The last line is just a trick to calculate the remaining length of the generator.
So this does not work for me if I am using this dataset in a deep learning framework where I need random access to the data in the dataset.

That use case is supported already. The one time password for bastion login would obviously need to be provided which means including some kind of user input for that part.

Eg:

```
hosts = ['server1.can.example.com', 'server2.can.example.com']

client = ParallelSSHClient(hosts, 
    proxy_host='bastion1.usa.example.com', 
    proxy_password='<one time bastion pass>')
```

Where `<one time bastion pass>` is the one time password token.

`proxy_host` is essentially ProxyCommand when the command is `ssh` but without the extra process and need for `ssh` binary. 

Since the functionality is different, however, it is not possible to use existing proxycommand configuration in `~/.ssh/config` to configure `proxy_host`. The equivalent would be `host_config` which does not support proxy configuration yet (#82).

Here are todos for next week's hacking session. These can become tickets if we decide to go this direction. Thoughts, @pde?

- [ ] Embed peep and add hashes of dependencies. (Maybe have individual requirements files for each package; maybe just have a big top-level one. TBD.)
- [ ] Do we need to keep the `pip install -U` of setuptools and pip? Possibly remove them. Virtualenv should give us usable versions by default: the earliest peep-compatible version of pip, 0.6.2, was included with virtualenv 1.4.4, released in January 2010.
- [ ] Either forcibly downgrade pip to <7 for peep compatibility, or fix peep.
- [ ] Replace insecure `pip install -U letsencrypt letsencrypt-apache` in letsencrypt-auto with peep. Run it against one or more requirements files from the network (either from our GitHub repo[1] or elsewhere). Sign the files for authentication, and check the signatures in letsencrypt-auto[2].
- [ ] Remove the redundant `pip install -U letsencrypt` that happens when we upgrade the nginx plugin. That's probably just a mistake.

Generally, letsencrypt-auto seems like a reasonable idea to me. We could try something fancier like [pex](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex) from Twitter, but I don't have any personal experience with it, we'd still need something to install OS packages, and it doesn't help us do automatic upgrades.

Per IRC tonight, we'll defer to OS packaging practices for the letsencrypt client when they get around to that. In the meantime, letsencrypt-auto will be our own little self-sufficient package manager.

[1] We could branch off master, add the signatures, then move a `release` branch to that commit. Downside: the branch would never fast-forward.

I think there are two things making me uneasy here:

* I don't feel like I have a very clear picture of what the goal here should be. Cancelling work is inherently a messy process, networks are unreliable, and in a distributed systems you rarely have complete information. Like in your example, you say you want to record the last record acknowledged by the remote WebSocket client, but for all you know there might be another acknowledgement that they already sent, just it's still sitting in a router somewhere and you haven't seen it yet. (This is the [Byzantine generals problem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Two_Generals%27_Problem).) I totally understand the desire to make this kind of thing as reliable as possible! I want that too. But in my experience, the first and most important step in making a distributed system reliable is to have a clear picture of what we {have to / are willing to} give up. Complexity is the number 1 enemy of reliability, so papering over individual problems as they arise just doesn't work. And even in your example of making a last call to a local database, I don't know how to judge whether the complexity cost is worth it. If the control-C had arrived 0.1 seconds earlier, then you wouldn't even know that there was a last record to write, you'd just have exited, and apparently that would have been OK – so is it really worth bending over backwards to handle it? Maybe it is, I don't know.

* As a matter of software design, I don't like the idea of low-level code imposing policy around timeouts and shutdowns. What if later on you decide that actually, on control-C exiting in 2 seconds is not good enough, you really need to exit in 1 second (or vice-versa). Do you have to go around and modify all the different places in your code that clean up resources?

  What if you're using several libraries, that each have their own internal policy on how long they'll hold out when you try to cancel them? What if you really want to shut down ASAP? Or on the other hand, what if one library wants to wait for 5 seconds and another wants to wait for 10 seconds, so the whole system ends up waiting for 10 seconds and that's fine, but the first library gave up after 5 seconds, and loses data that could have been saved if it had known that it actually had 10 seconds to work with?

These two concerns are closely related, because if we're hard-coding timeouts into little pieces of the code that can't see the big picture, then we're very likely to make the wrong trade-offs; OTOH if timeouts are being configured as late as possible and at as high a level as possible, then it's much more plausible that we can make the right trade-offs. 

I almost feel like what we want is for a top-down cancellation to be able to specify a "grace period" – so the code that calls `cancel()` can also specify how long it's willing to wait for the cancellation to complete. The problem then would be in communicating this to the code being cancelled. The simple version would be:

* by default, code is immediately cancelled
* but code that wants to allow for graceful shutdown can have something like a "soft shield" that shields against cancellation during the grace period, but not after the grace period expires
* and there's some mechanism that code running under the "soft shield" can check whether a cancellation has occurred, so that it knows that it should shut down the connection or whatever, e.g. doing `if currently_cancelled(): ...` or something.

That's enough for cases where the connection is *active*, so e.g. we can imagine a HTTP server that every time it goes to send a response, it checks for `currently_cancelled()` and if so then it marks the response as being the last one of this connection and immediately closes it afterwards. But what it's missing is some way for the code that wants to support graceful cleanup to get *notified* when a cancellation happens – like if an HTTP server or your websocket server is sitting idle waiting for something to arrive, then when it's cancelled it should immediately wake up and shut down the connection without having to poll `currently_cancelled()`.

One obvious way to do this would be to send in an exception, and then code that wants to handle it gracefully can catch it and do whatever clever thing it wants. But... in general, you *can't* necessarily catch a `Cancelled` exception and do something clever with it. (This is an important point that I missed before, but is relevant to what you're trying to do in general!). The mere fact of delivering a `Cancelled` exception can itself break your connection irrecoverably. Like, consider if you were in the middle of doing a `sendall` to transmit a websocket message frame, and that got interrupted – you can't necessarily catch that exception and send a goodbye frame, because the previous frame might have been only half-transmitted and now you've lost synchronization. A `Cancelled` exception is better behaved than a generic `KeyboardInterrupt`, but it's still a bomb going off in the middle of your program state.

Maybe it's enough to make the soft-shield state toggleable, so you could do something like:

```python
async def handler(ws_conn):
    with soft_shield_enabled:
        while True:
            try:
                with soft_shield_disabled:
                    msg = await ws_conn.next_message()
            except Cancelled:
                await do_cleanup(ws_conn)
                return
            await ws_conn.send_message(make_response(msg))
```

(Possibly interesting trivia: I stole the `hazmat` name from [the `cryptography` library](https://cryptography.io/en/latest/#layout).)

Anyway... it sounds like in your setup, you have something like a continuously monitoring sensor, and you want to send regular snapshots to a central server? and you want to optimize for metrics like, average staleness of the server's information, worst case staleness of the server's information, etc.? I think the best design here really depends a lot on the details: the approach of sending one, waiting for acknowledgement, sending another, etc. loop gives you a system where the server's staleness varies from 0.5x RTT to 1.5x RTT, with an average of 1 RTT and the upper bound being tight. If that's good enough for your application then it's hard to beat the simplicity :-).

OTOH the best you could possibly hope for is to keep the server's staleness down to 0.5x RTT (which is what you get if you send continuously, but *just* slowly enough that no messages back up), so if you need something better than that you had better find a way to change the problem. And if you really need something in between, then you might need to get clever.

I guess another very simple strategy would be to send a packet every N milliseconds on a timer; then your staleness varies between 0.5x RTT and (0.5x RTT + N milliseconds), where you choose N small enough to meet your staleness requirements while being large enough that in practice it never overwhelms your network connection. (But if the server becomes continuously overloaded, or the network overwhelmed, then it's possible that congestion would occur anyway.)

That makes sense. I think by default when you create a TensorFlow session, it will try to reserve space on all of the visible GPUs, and so if you have multiple processes trying to do this then some of them may fail.

You can do something like the following.

```python
import os

@ray.actor(num_gpus=1)
class myactor(object):
    def __init__(self, model_config): 
        os.environ['CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES'] = ','.join([str(i) for i in ray.get_gpu_ids()])
        from keras.utils.layer_utils import layer_from_config
        self.model = layer_from_config(model_config, custom_objects={})
```

Then you should be able to create four actors (since you have four GPUs).

```python
a1 = myactor()
a2 = myactor()
a3 = myactor()
a4 = myactor()
```

Then if you try to create a fifth actor `a5 = myactor()`, an exception will be raised saying there aren't enough GPUs. At least that's the current behavior.

Note that this all assumes you started ray with `ray.init(num_gpus=4)`.

If you want to create actors that don't use any CPUs, you could do the following.

```python
class myactor(object):
    def __init__(self, model_config): 
        os.environ['CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES'] = ','.join([str(i) for i in ray.get_gpu_ids()])
        from keras.utils.layer_utils import layer_from_config
        self.model = layer_from_config(model_config, custom_objects={})

gpu_actor = ray.actor(num_gpus=1)(myactor)
cpu_actor = ray.actor(myactor)

# Then create some GPU actors and some CPU actors
gpu_actors = [gpu_actor() for _ in range(4)]
cpu_actors = [cpu_actor() for _ in range(10)]
```

Ok I will try to be more explicit.

CRFsuite uses transition features of the form `I(y_t-1=a, y_t=b)` and state features of the form `f(x) I(y_t=a)`. For transitions, the features are automatically created. 

With `word2features()` you specify `f(x)` in state features. For a feature like `word.istitle()`, the logs potentials look like `I(x.istitle()=True/False)*I(y_t=a)` where a take each possible value for label.

The feature `biais` does not depend of `x` so by adding `biais` you are adding in your set of feature all the features of the form `cte*I(y_t=a)` where `a` has any value of a label.

So the model is learning those weights associated with labels as if labels where draw independently from a given probability distribution.

This is fixed in https://github.com/wireservice/csvkit/pull/781 which hasn't been merged because it's backwards incompatible, so is waiting for an appropriate release.

I'm not clear on how setting an output delimiter using an option in every csvkit command changes the situation compared to piping that output to `csvformat` and using the `-D` option to set the output delimiter at that point. For the examples of:

* unquoted comma-containing fields
* unmatched quote characters
* carriage returns and/or line feeds in unquoted text fields
* spaces in space-delimited text fields

csvkit cannot guess which of the alternative interpretations of the data is correct when reading that input. If there is a field value containing an unquoted comma, csvkit can't determine whether that row has one more column than other rows, or whether one of the fields contains an unquoted comma - and it definitely can't determine which of those fields contains that comma. Each of the examples above produces alternative interpretations, either of which may be correct. In such cases, you need a human to make the decision, and either code those decisions into a custom script, or correct the file manually. csvkit just reads CSVs using the rules of Python's csv module, while allowing for some of the [variations](https://docs.python.org/2/library/csv.html#dialects-and-formatting-parameters) that Python's csv module supports.

Going back to the unquoted comma-containing field example, I don't see how setting the output delimiter helps the situation. If you have a row `country,1,234,567,2013` - that should be read as `country` has a population of `1,234,567` in the year `2013` - running `csvcut -c 1-5 -D =` (using the proposed output delimiter option `-D`) will read it as five values, and will output `country=1=234=567=2013`. Running `csvcut -c 1-2 -D =` (because you only want the country and population) will output `country=1`, which isn't what you want, but it is the best csvkit can do. But in either case, having the `-D` option on `csvcut` instead of `csvformat` doesn't change the output...

> As Django's default TestCase uses transactions all tests with DB could be speed up

pytest-django uses it as well in `db` fixture. `TransactionalTestCase` logic is used in `transactional_db` fixture. Thus being said, there will be no performance difference regarding DB queries.
Relevant implementation is here - https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-django/blob/master/pytest_django/fixtures.py#L121

> I think (real) fixtures are bad: difficult to maintain, I really prefer factories (from factory boy),

Fixtures are a great example of the dependency injection pattern, which idea is to separate implementation and simplify testing. Also, fixtures in pytest are not only about objects creation. They are flexible in the way that you could use factory boy factory, some custom generator, basically, anything and you're not forced to choose some exact approach. You could call factory boy factories in it or create some complicated setup and then reuse it or parametrize. In what way is it difficult to maintain? Have you tried `pytest-factoryboy`?

> Reusable fixtures and parametrization are mock out, it could be done with a decorator from `mock.patch`

Could you provide a link to the decorator you mentioned? Would it yield different test cases when applied to a test? Would it provide some caching for expensive set-ups?

> plain assert usage: Yup of course

Does it provide an extended introspection as pytest does?

> But in practice, it's a bit hard deal with a mix of django.test and pytest. Too much question just to create some test:

I don't think, that these questions are recurring, it is more like that they will be solved after reading pytest documentation. More important questions are about testing logic and quality of the result of writing a test. It is just an instrument to make things. When you know how to use it - you will not have questions like this.

> I think pytest is a good tool as launcher, but as library, it doesn't provide any particular thing in a Django app (compared to Django+StdLib)

Django has a lot of really cool testing tools - it would be nice to have more assertions from the test cases (https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-django/pull/232). But pytest itself has quite a lot, as I mentioned before (parametrization on all python versions, fixtures (including generator-based + autouse feature)), but the most important is the flexibility it has - you can write function-based tests or use a default Python's `unittest`, or from Django. In general, the tests could be cleaner, since you just type less. 

I don't want to write about all this stuff here, but it definitely worth checking - https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/contents.html

> I'm voluntary to make this job alone and propose a big PR ;)

I doubt that it will ever be merged. Because the motivation is not objective and is a matter of personal taste. More likely it will reduce the flexibility we have there without any valuable benefit. At least I don't see any now. If you'll provide some examples of how the test suite will be better and why - it would be a good starting point for the discussion. Let's start with a concrete, incremental changes with concrete benefits instead of a single big pull request. For example - factory boy could be helpful in our test suite, for sure, but it needs to be checked.

From my perspective it is not a big priority at the moment, I think there are a couple of things in our issues list that could be done much easier and add more benefit (it is more clear at least) to the end user.

Cheers

The best algorithm should be put forward when several are available with a similar naming convention. If a user wants marching cubes, they should get our best marching cubes by running `marching_cubes` - not needing to dive into the literature or even docstrings to figure out `*_lewiner` is superior. We may not always be truly Pythonic with "one best way," because in science alternatives for comparison are important, but *we should make the best way the easiest way*.

Unfortunately, due to past choices this is not always possible without either a years-long deprecation or a breaking change. Some disagree with me on this. but I very strongly feel we should make the breaking change in such cases. Our API needs it.

So I'm in favor of option A, with a tweak. In addition to the release notes, we could add a deprecation warning to the name `marching_cubes` which tells users something like "A better,, more efficient algorithm is now used which now returns four instead of two outputs including normals (see docstring for details). Exact backwards compatibility with 0.12 and prior, is available with `marching_cubes_classic`." IMHO a breaking change with instruction is entirely different than slapping users with sudden `ValueError: too many values to unpack`

I do not think is is possible to install skimage without compiling the binaries.  It possible to install a version where the compiled binaries cannot be imported.  For example installing a wheel or conda package for the incorrect architecture (there are checks to prevent this but it can be done).  Also, having a shared library linked against a newer GLIB version than is available on the system (happens often with poorly built Linux conda packages).

The question is, do we need an overly helpful error message in these cases.  These errors should not be common, at least not as common as importing from the source tree, so I think a catch all "your install seems to be broken, re-install" should suffice.

In short, I have difficulties to understand how we can promote free/libre softwares (python, scipy and scikits) in one hand and in the other hand we can rely proprietary softwares or components that we cannot trust (either in terms for privacy (GA) or control and perennity (travis-ci, google mailing lists)). I'm really disappointed that those questions are almost never addressed in the community, but it's not the right place to argue further.
I am still not clear on the advantage on annotating all functions with types at this point in time.
I should justify the removal of the bias argument further: if you repeatedly smooth the histogram, it is quite unlikely you will end up with flat basins.  As such, the bias argument makes less sense.
This is not relevant anymore (see https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/1933). Closing.

Hi @simflin, thanks for the report!

It's unclear whether it's a bug or a feature. The behaviour changed in ea40a555, because of a report that `label2rgb` couldn't handle negative labels. Whoops!

We'll probably have to have a discussion about adding a keyword argument to control this behaviour, but in the meantime, your problem is actually the easiest possible case for label2rgb, and you could write your own one-liner:

```python
def label2rgb_direct(labels, colors):
    return colors[labels.ravel()].reshape(labels.shape + (3,))
```

You could also remap your colors to be sequential also:

```python
from skimage.segmentation import relabel_sequential
_, _, inv = relabel_sequential(labels)
new_colors = colors[inv]
```

I'm not sure this matters very much. One is a constant factor from the other, and neither appears to be guaranteed to be in the range of values recommended for skimage datatypes. Do you know whether absolute values make sense in this scale?

@jakirkham: No, as I mentioned in the other thread, `qualname` can't handle a lot of cases… what you are seeing is the author's choice that when `inspect` fails to fabricate a line of code that will throw an `AttributeErrror` with reference to `__qualname__`.

Bad keys can be often removed with `del archive[bad_key]` or one of the 'private' methods like `archive._rmdir(bad_key)`. 

Keys that can be added to the archive, but are (for example) unhashable and thus can't be used in a dict view of the archive... should either not be allowed, or possibly more favorably, the 'dict' view of the archive should allow unhashable types.

Keys that are bad (but in the archive) should have a method that enables them to be detected (like `archive.unhashable_keys()` or `archive.bad_keys()` or something similar.

We should also be able to identify bad entries with `archive.bad_entries()` or something similar.

More care needs to be taken to not allow bad keys to be added, or if bad keys/values/items are in the archive, then it should be easy to detect and purge them (i.e. `archive.clean()`).

Some good ideas, here are my thoughts:

- colors should be listed, I think, as actual colors (black, red, or something else). There isn't really any cost to collecting this information.

- I don't know about the `provider` key of codepages. `iconv` is platform specific. In Python, we'd just use their builtin encoding engine (which may are may not use iconv, I don't know). The point being, these encodings are standardized, and implementors are free to convert them however they like. Non-standard encodings we can provide with a `data` key. If there is no `data` and the implementor doesn't have any other source, he can't encode. So no need for `none` either. I would just allow an optional `data` key.

- In designing my attempt at a YAML format, I noticed that some printer have different column configurations. TM-P80 has a "42 column emulation mode", and TM-P60II 2's columns depend on the paper size. My approach was to define the different "column configs" and then basically in code letting the user pass some arguments to the profile, i.e. TM80Profile(emulation_mode=True). In any case, there should be some way we can store this information.

  I think an idea I like much more now is to simply define - for those cases - two profiles. "TM-P80 (42-column emulation)" could be that.

That brings me to the last point. I'm not sure about the `printers` list. I gather you want to define not a profile for each printer, like I did, but have base profiles that link to the printers.

Now you know the printer ecosystem better than me, but my impression at least is that there are bound to be a bunch of models out there that have the same command set, but may differ in columns or media size. So I think we might end up with something like this:

     TM80Profile:
         printers:
             - TM80

On a deeper level, I am worried that this format encourages us to attempt to merge duplicate information in shared profiles that really should be duplicated and stored with the printer model. In other words, if there are three printers with the same `media/width` configuration, I would argue that this should be written out three separate times. There should not be a `Foo` profile that tries to accommodate a set of printer models that are not really related, except that their meda size happens to be the same. 

I'm not sure if I'm explaining this right. I'm afraid the profiles and printers get muddled up.

For example, I don't think the `default` profile should contain any information about columns or media size. As an implementor,  should be able to tell, from the data, whether the column or width information I have is just a fallback default, or actual information.

I imagine that printers could be a first class data structure; they do "inherit" from profiles in the sense that both can contain the same keys. Maybe the printer would allow some extra information like the manufacturer to be stored.

     printers:
        TM80P:
             inherits: default
             media:
                width:
                    mm: 72

With OpenGL you can't do rendering asynchronously, all OpenGL calls must be on the same thread.
