Hi @GustavZ,

I will close this first. If you still have any issue, you can re-open it or create another ticket.

That makes sense to have post processing on by default since, in terms or spatial resolution, I cannot really distinguish between 1280 x 720 with decimation filter off and 640 x 360 with decimation filter on. Depth noise is less with the filter on which is an advantage. If I instead compare the spatial resolution between a set resolution of 640 x 360 without decimation and 640 x 360 with decimation (i.e., set resolution of 1280 x 720), the latter case has better spatial resolution. So it is perhaps a little unfair to nominally downgrade the resolution when activating the decimation filter since it does not seem to be the same thing as actually reducing the spatial resolution.

The depth resolution scales with resolution mode and with distance and with camera. 
I will here talk about the RMS error (the depth noise of a plane). I will also assume you have a well textured plane you are looking at. (The projector clearly works to create texture as well, but you do get better results passively IF there is a texture already).

So for D435, in 848x480, the minimum operating distance (minZ) is 17cm. At 20cm, the RMS error should theoretically be <0.3mm. So will you see this?  You can, but you need to make sure you have a flat surface of course. Then you need to change the depth units. By default the camera reports depth in 1000um steps, or 1mm.  You can change this under the Advanced Mode to 100um for example. Finally, there is the issue that we currently focus the lenses with hyperfocal at 50cm. Which means images will be a bit blurred at 20cm, so the actual depth degrade a bit. 

For the D415, at 1280x720, the minZ is 44cm. So you you to see anything at 20cm you have two options. You can reduce the resolution to 640x360 and your minZ is 22cm. Or go to 320x240 and the minZ is 11cm. At 20cm the RMS error will be ~200um. Again, there will be the same caveats as stated before. 

For a stereo system the RMS error will scale quadratically. Another way of saying this is that the % error (which is % of distance) will scale linearly. So if you have 1% error at 4m, then you will have 2% at 8m, and 4% at 16m. And conversely you will have 0.5% at 2m, and 0.25% at 1m.   
    
One last thing to think about: while I have mentioned measuring a plane at 20cm away, since we are ranging based on stereo vision, we will start having more serious occlusion issues when a real object (not a plane) gets closer. Think of a finger approaching your eyes. At some point your left eye will only see the right side of the finger and the right eye will only see the left side, so there will not be any correspondance matching between left and right images. And you get a real head ache. The ASIC does too. 

I hope this helps.

Hi @tcsavage,

Even if returns `None` will still be non-compliant with the spec (as it requires the absence of the attribute, raising `AttributeError` should be the correct behavior), *nullability* is still different than *undefinability*.

However, there are different reasons this is developed the current way:
* Ease of appending errors in the `ExecutionResult`.
* The clients (`flask-graphql`, `graphene-django`, `sanic-graphql`, ...) handle this automatically by adding a errors field *only* when [there are errors in the execution result](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-server-core/blob/master/graphql_server/__init__.py#L148).
* `bool(execution_result.errors)` will still be `False` for an empty array.

From the issue description, it seems none of this server packages are being used, which are required if you want to expose a "spec-compliant" GraphQL endpoint and the ones that handle this special case for you.

@simonklb interesting thoughts around a complex topic.  We've wrestled with the same issues in the (reactive) OpenStack charms; we discussed the issue of using (effectively) 'guard' states to stop execution, and then went with the idea of idempotency of handlers to accept the fact that lots of handlers would run again with every hook that run.

However, this is particularly a problem with the update-status hook as 'nothing' has changed, yet the handlers all get the chance to run again.  Nothing 'should' change, but in a few instances, we've had services restart every 5 minutes due to an unintended non-idempotent handler causing the restart due to 'something'!

We've mused whether update-status should be handled separately (i.e. NOT start reactive) ... but, all those handlers running results in busy log files.

The trade-off is a simpler-to-write charm  (as long as the idempotency is handled correctly) for a busier log and more 'work' being done each time a hook is fired, with concomitant effects due to many, many calls into the local juju charm functions.

What I don't want to add is more hidden complexity in how the states/flags change.  And that's going to be a delicate balance between explicitness and power.

I like the behavior that if not in DOM than not visible.  I was using it this way as it made some logic simpler.

For example on the embedded systems I test on some are not very powerful; it can take up to 10 secs to generate keys for https negotiation.  This can causes delays like this:
- I load a TLS cert to DUT to be used for https.  
- The GUI will return success but the DUT is restarting its web server 
- So the GUI often will not succeed in making a background polling API call to DUT during this.
- Which causes GUI to go modal with 'popup' of lost contact
- My test will fail since it cannot navigate away to start next test.

In this case my logic waits an empirically determined time than checks to see if lost contact pop-up is there.  This logic started failing on the faster embedded h/w with SeleniumLibrary 3.0.0 because the pop-up never shows up.

I moved to kw 'Wait Until Page Does Not Contain Element' for fix but I am concerned this will fail when I start testing on the slower h/w Because of the way our SPA GUI works;   If a popup is used and then removed thru click or change in state (polling succeeded) its html is left in the DOM but marked so it is not visible. The next time some event causes a complete page redrawn the old pop-up html is removed.

So if Wait...Not Visible fails when not in DOM I will have to change logic to:
- Check if ID in DOM, if so then wail till not visible.

Workable but not as simple.

> You can definitely bulk create resources, just not as part of a relationship payload.

Current parser does not allow bulk creating resources. It expects the data to be dict. It will actually cause 500 HTTP errors when data is an array (`data.get('type')`).

JSON-API allows extensions, but these are experimental for now. Bulk operations used to be one of the official extensions before the whole extension system was sent back to the drawing board.

You can see the original bulk spec here:
https://github.com/json-api/json-api/blob/9c7a03dbc37f80f6ca81b16d444c960e96dd7a57/extensions/bulk/index.md

I have a need for bulk operations in my app and I've implemented a few custom views to handle them. I don't mind that django-rest-framework-json-api does not come with these views out of the box, but I would like the parser not to reject these types of requests.

Even though there's no spec for handling bulk operations yet, the official spec does allow this kind of payload:

> Primary data MUST be either:
> 
> * a single resource object, a single resource identifier object, or null, for requests that target single resources
> * an array of resource objects, an array of resource identifier objects, or an empty array ([]), for requests that target resource collections

> Sure, bibtex will issue a warning (no error, btw.)

Perhaps those users that use permissive tool chain see a warning and don't complain, while those users that use restrictive tool chain see an error and complain :smile: 

I'm mentioning this because you can find several sources on the web saying that `volume` is optional, not mandatory, for `@article`.  This contradicts the [wikipedia entry](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BibTeX) cited by @lnielsen.  And, as far as I'm concerned, it makes more sense to me for `volume` to be optional, as this nicely covers the "submitted-to" paper scenario, where the volume may not be known until the paper is published several months later.

For additional insight, let me quote from the "BibTeXing" 1988 guide by Oren Patashnik himself:

- **article** An article from a journal or magazine. Required fields: author, title, journal, year. Optional fields: volume, number, pages, month, note.

(You can find this guide somewhere under ` /usr/share/doc/texlive-doc/bibtex/base/btxdoc.pdf` in your TeX installation.)

I therefore agree with @aw-bib that `volume` is really an optional field that does not justify switching `@article` type to `@misc` in our BibTeX output.  (While missing `journal` would be another matter.)

Anyway, this discussion just shows that each site may want to configure their TeX output to follow the habits of their user community and perhaps their prevalent tool chain.  Invenio-wise, we can come with good defaults in our demo site, following standards as closely as possible, all the while offering easily configurable/extendible hooks, letting admins to tweak the output further if they wish.  (E.g. consider where to store URLs and abstracts and stuff that the standard does not mention.  We may follow majority usage in our subject domain, but this won't necessarily suit everybody.)

Hi @saikirankv, this is the way Pact is designed. It follows Postel's law about being strict with what you send out (so every part of the request must be "expected") and relaxed with what you accept (so the request can have extra elements that can be safely ignored). If you are finding the test maintenance too high, then perhaps you are using Pact in the wrong way. Please read this best practice guideline for using Pact https://docs.pact.io/best-practices/consumer#use-pact-for-isolated-unit-tests

you don't need to extend the `%PATH%` environment variable yourself, just do `pip install afdko` from within the activated virtual environment, or install globally outside the venv with `python -m pip install afdko`.

Calling the activate.bat script will ensure the venv's Scripts folder is at the beginning of the PATH, so typing `makeotf` will just work.
When you install to the global python environment, the Scripts folder of the latter must be in your `%PATH%`, if it isn't you won't be able to call makeotf or any other scripts.

If you pip install with `--user` flag (globally but not for all users, only for you), the scripts will be installed somewhere else (check pip docs, I forgot where).

# See https://github.com/facebookresearch/fastText/blob/master/get-wikimedia.sh
#
# From https://github.com/facebookresearch/fastText/issues/161:
#
# We now have a script called 'get-wikimedia.sh', that you can use to download and
# process a recent wikipedia dump of any language. This script applies the preprocessing
# we used to create the published word vectors.
#
# The parameters we used to build the word vectors are the default skip-gram settings,
# except with a dimensionality of 300 as indicated on the top of the list of word
# vectors (we now understand that this could be more visible).

'''
        sed -e "s/’/'/g" -e "s/′/'/g" -e "s/''/ /g" -e "s/'/ ' /g" -e "s/“/\"/g" -e "s/”/\"/g" \
            -e 's/"/ " /g' -e 's/\./ \. /g' -e 's/<br \/>/ /g' -e 's/, / , /g' -e 's/(/ ( /g' -e 's/)/ ) /g' -e 's/\!/ \! /g' \
            -e 's/\?/ \? /g' -e 's/\;/ /g' -e 's/\:/ /g' -e 's/-/ - /g' -e 's/=/ /g' -e 's/=/ /g' -e 's/*/ /g' -e 's/|/ /g' \
            -e 's/«/ /g' | tr 0-9 " "
'''
SUBEXES = ["s/’/'/g", "s/′/'/g", "s/''/ /g", "s/'/ ' /g", 's/“/"/g', 's/”/"/g', 's/"/ /g', "s/\\./ \\. /g", "s/<br \\/>/ /g", "s/, / , /g", "s/(/ ( /g", "s/)/ ) /g", "s/\\!/ \\! /g", "s/\\?/ \\? /g", "s/\\;/ /g", "s/\\:/ /g", "s/-/ - /g", "s/=/ /g", "s/=/ /g", "s/*/ /g", "s/|/ /g"], "s/«/ /g"]

import subprocess

def __normalize_text(s):
    for subex in SUBEXES:
        print("Applying", subex)
        s = subprocess.check_output(['sed', subex], input=s.encode()).decode("utf-8")
        print(s)
    return s

# Program to filter Wikipedia XML dumps to "clean" text consisting only of lowercase
# letters (a-z, converted from A-Z), and spaces (never consecutive)...
# All other characters are converted to spaces.  Only text which normally appears.
# in the web browser is displayed.  Tables are removed.  Image captions are.
# preserved.  Links are converted to normal text.  Digits are spelled out.
# *** Modified to not spell digits or throw away non-ASCII characters ***
# Written by Matt Mahoney, June 10, 2006.  This program is released to the public domain.

def __spaces(s):
    return ' '.join(s.split())

def preproc(s):
    s = s.lower()
    s = __normalize_text(s)
    s = __spaces(s)
    return s
```

In marshmallow 3, Schema is always `strict`. You get a `ValidationError` if something goes wrong during deserialization. You can access the correctly-deserialized data as an attribute of the exception (`exc.valid_data`), but `post_load` is skipped like in MA2.

We seem to agree that calling `post_load` on only successful items without removing failed items would result in inconsistent types.

Removing an item because it partially failed would be problematic, because you'd be losing data, so it can't be the default behaviour. Also, in a list, the order and the index may matter and if you remove an element, you shift all the indexes.

I just did a test by replacing `Nested(many=True)` with `List(Nested())`. In this case, it is even worse, the whole data from the `List` field is considered invalid and there is nothing at all in `exc.valid_data` (https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/766).

It appears that https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/pull/262 attempts to solve the same issue.

Hawk does allow for optional content signing and file uploads is the main use case. To send requests with mohawk, you have to [explicitly turn off content signing](http://mohawk.readthedocs.org/en/latest/usage.html#skipping-content-checks). The server must also be configured to allow unsigned content. I think it is the default in Node to allow unsigned content but if a server is using mohawk it will need to explicitly allow unsigned content. 

When turning content signing off you still get signed headers so the security may be good enough. You also still have TLS to sign all content, of course.

> I don't think this is quite right. The pixel shape depends on the total
>  charge stored in the pixel and is independent of where in the pixel the
>  photons landed. Arun's suggestion is a good one for deciding whether a
>  photon stays in the undistorted pixel or moves to a neighboring pixel. If
>  it lands near the center, then it is unlikely to move to a neighboring
>  pixel, and there might be a way to use this information to speed up the
>  decision of whether to move the photon or not. But I don't think it impacts
>  the decision of when to update the pixel shapes. Hope this makes sense
> I was not suggesting that the update itself depends on where the photons
> land - I was thinking that when we update the pixel boundaries can depend
> on the location of the photon. For each incoming photon, if it falls in the
> central region of a pixel, then irrespective of the boundary distortions,
> we can be confident that the photon is not going to contribute to the
> electron count in any other pixel. We do not have to update the pixel shape
> after we receive this photon. We can keep track of the number of photons we
> have received since our previous update. Once a photon falls dangerously
> close to the pixel boundary, say 10% of pixel size away from the edges of
> the undistorted square pixels (which happens with probability of  ~20% and
> hence 5x speed up?), then we update the pixel shape boundary using the
> photon counts we have and then decide which pixel the latest photon falls
> into. I think this is valid way to reduce the number of calls to new
> subroutines.

There's a flag in there (```use_bulgefit```) that says whether the b+d fit is preferred over Sersic from a statistical perspective.  If the flag says that b+d is not preferred, then the flux and hlr values were not precomputed and stored, so you should get 0.  (The b+d fit parameters are still available in the files, so you can use them if you want; I just didn't have it store hlr and flux in these arrays for these cases.) So when you are doing this test, you should check that ```use_bulgefit``` is 1 before you use the precomputed hlr and flux values for b+d fits.

I think it's 100% safe to remove anything that is no longer supported upstream. I do it pretty religiously in all my libraries.

Regarding dropping 1.8 compatibility, it's of course trickier since it's still supported until next April. However, 1.8 is supported on a security-only basis. It is imho completely reasonable to drop support for it in a point-release, and, if necessary, do minor security releases on the older release should the need arise. It's also a lot simpler to deal with than a bunch of compat hacks.

Additionally, polymorphic is imho a case of a fairly complex library which deals with Django's inner workings quite a lot. Django has changed a bunch since 1.8, and supporting it in the most recent release increases surface area a lot. You can see it in the commit stats: `+236 insertions, -912 deletions`.

Finally, Django themselves recommended that once Django 2.0 is out, libraries drop support for everything except Django 1.11 and 2.0. It's not out yet but it's a decent idea to prepare for it.

I would recommend landing this PR, as well as a cleaned up #282 (I can help with it), and do a new major release shortly after (1.3 or 2.0, whatever semver you think is worth bumping for dropping so many versions of Django at once). Put the previous major branch in security-only maintenance.

@ehdsouza thanks for the quick response! Actually I have figured out the issue. I failed to see the changes in the API's from 0.26.0 to 1.6.0 For example:

### imports
**v0.26.0**
```
import watson_developer_cloud
import watson_developer_cloud.natural_language_understanding.features.v1 as features
```
**v1.6.0**
```
from watson_developer_cloud import NaturalLanguageUnderstandingV1
from watson_developer_cloud.natural_language_understanding_v1 \
    import Features, EntitiesOptions, KeywordsOptions, ConceptsOptions, CategoriesOptions, EmotionOptions, MetadataOptions, RelationsOptions, SemanticRolesOptions, SentimentOptions

```

### Also getting client has changed:
**v0.26.0**
```
nlu = watson_developer_cloud.NaturalLanguageUnderstandingV1(
    version=version, username=username, password=password)
```

**v1.6.0**
```
nlu = NaturalLanguageUnderstandingV1(
    version=version, username=username, password=password)
```

### And getting the features has changed as well...

**v0.26.0**
```
feature = features.SemanticRoles(
            entities=entities, keywords=keywords, limit=limit)
```

**v1.6.0**
```
feature = Features(semantic_roles=SemanticRolesOptions(
            entities=entities, keywords=keywords, limit=limit))
```


After these updates my application is working fine again, hopefully this helps someone else.
